Brazil is Latin America's largest satellite market, driven by a vast territory, uneven terrestrial infrastructure, and a television-hungry population of 215 million people.

41911.d: The second TJS satellite, continuing China's military GEO communications expansion under the experimental designation. TJS-2's position in geostationary orbit provides it with continuous coverage of a fixed geographic region — consistent with military communications support for Chinese forces, intelligence collection, or data relay functions. The rapid pace of TJS launches suggests an urgent military operational requirement.

41937.d: The fourth SBIRS missile warning satellite, positioned to provide redundant coverage of the most likely missile threat corridors. SBIRS has been operational during North Korean ICBM tests, Iranian missile strikes on US bases in Iraq, and numerous other launch events — providing US commanders with crucial seconds to minutes of warning that change the calculus of every military confrontation.

41942.d: Spain's advanced all-electric satellite for the Americas and Europe, pioneering all-electric propulsion for a major commercial GEO satellite. All-electric satellites use ion engines for orbit raising instead of chemical rockets — taking months to reach GEO instead of weeks but saving thousands of kilograms of propellant mass that translates into more payload capacity or lower launch costs. Hispasat 36W-1 demonstrated that all-electric GEO satellites are commercially viable, accelerating their adoption across the industry.

41948.d: One of India's high-cadence Cartosat-2 series satellites providing continuous sub-meter mapping imagery. The series demonstrates India's mature space manufacturing capability — producing complex optical imaging satellites on an annual schedule with high reliability, supporting both civilian infrastructure and defense mapping requirements.

42063.d: Sentinel-2B flies offset 180 degrees from Sentinel-2A — together achieving global land coverage every 5 days. Five days is fast enough to catch a spreading forest fire, a flooding river, or a glacier calving an iceberg. The combined archive is the world's most-downloaded free satellite dataset, used by hundreds of thousands of researchers and applications daily.

42075.d: The ninth WGS satellite with enhanced polar coverage extending into the Arctic Circle. WGS-9 was the first funded entirely by a coalition of international partners — Australia, Canada, Denmark, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, and New Zealand collectively funded and own capacity on the satellite. A genuine allied investment in shared military communications infrastructure.

42662.d: China's first commercial Ka-band high-throughput satellite, officially designated Shijian-13 in its experimental phase before becoming operational ChinaSat-16. It was China's technology validation for next-generation broadband satellite technology — the first Chinese satellite to use electric propulsion for orbit raising, reducing launch mass significantly. It demonstrated Ka-band broadband delivery at performance levels previously only achievable by Western commercial operators.

42691.d: South Korea's Ka-band high-throughput satellite, providing broadband internet across Korea and Southeast Asia. KT SAT's KoreaSat-7 introduced high-throughput Ka-band broadband service for Korean domestic customers and expanded into Southeast Asian markets — Indonesia, the Philippines, and Vietnam — where satellite broadband fills critical gaps in national connectivity infrastructure. Korea's sophisticated communications technology sector drives the development of increasingly capable satellite platforms.

42692.d: Brazil's first dedicated military communications satellite — the Geostationary Defense and Strategic Communications Satellite. SGDC provides encrypted X-band military communications for the Brazilian armed forces and Ka-band broadband internet for Brazil's national broadband connectivity program. Brazil combined military and civilian missions to reduce costs while achieving defense requirements for sovereign communications independent of commercial providers.

42695.d: India's gift to its South Asian neighbors — the South Asia Satellite launched as a diplomatic initiative providing satellite connectivity to India's neighboring nations. GSAT-9 provides Ku-band services to Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, the Maldives, Nepal, and Sri Lanka as a free Indian offering. The satellite supports broadcasting, weather services, broadband connectivity, and telemedicine across some of South Asia's smallest and least-connected nations — diplomatic power expressed in satellite form.

42698.d: The fourth Global Xpress satellite, expanding capacity in high-demand regions. Inmarsat-5 F4 added coverage redundancy and capacity to support growing in-flight broadband demand as more airlines equipped their fleets with satellite Wi-Fi. The exponential growth of passenger expectations for connectivity — driven by smartphone adoption and streaming services — made each successive Global Xpress satellite necessary sooner than Inmarsat had originally planned.

42738.d: The second Michibiki quasi-zenith satellite, providing GPS augmentation across the Asia-Oceania region. Michibiki-2 carries centimeter-level augmentation signals that correct GPS errors in real time — enabling the precision agriculture, autonomous construction equipment, and self-driving vehicles that Japan is deploying across its territory. The Michibiki L6 signal provides free sub-centimeter positioning corrections that would otherwise cost premium subscription fees on commercial correction networks.

42740.d: The successor to ViaSat-1, with nearly 300 Gbps capacity and coverage extending from the continental US to Mexico, Central America, and northern South America. ViaSat-2 also pioneered aviation broadband — providing fast satellite internet to commercial aircraft over North America and the Atlantic through JetBlue, United, and other airlines. ViaSat-2 made in-flight Wi-Fi fast enough to stream video reliably, permanently changing passenger expectations of commercial aviation.

42747.d: India's first Ka-band high-throughput satellite, launched as the first mission of India's heavy-lift GSLV Mk III rocket. GSAT-19 demonstrated both Ka-band satellite technology and India's new large rocket in a single mission — two firsts for India's space program. It carries propulsion experiments and space environment instrumentation alongside its communication payload, providing valuable data for future Indian satellite and launch vehicle design.

42758.d: China's first X-ray astronomy satellite — Huiyan means 'Insight' or 'Sharp Eyes' — scans the sky for X-ray sources from black holes, neutron stars, and gamma-ray bursts. In 2017, just three months after launch, it detected the electromagnetic counterpart to a gravitational wave event (GW170817), the collision of two neutron stars 130 million light-years away. This observation marked China's arrival at the frontier of multi-messenger astronomy.

42767.d: Continuing India's steady cadence of high-resolution Earth observation, Cartosat-2E ensures no gap in India's national mapping program. The series demonstrates how a consistent satellite production pipeline — rather than occasional large missions — builds the most valuable long-term Earth observation archives.

42814.d: A Greek-led communications satellite serving southeastern Europe and extending coverage to South Asia. Hellas-Sat 3 was built in partnership with Israel Aerospace Industries and jointly owned by Greek and Saudi interests — a commercial collaboration that brought diverse investment to expand Greek satellite capacity. It serves broadcasting, broadband, and maritime communications across the Mediterranean and Middle East.

42829.d: Built by students at TU Berlin, TechnoSat tests miniaturized satellite components under actual space flight conditions. Its passive attitude control system and experimental sensors have been validated on orbit and feed directly into Germany's next generation of small satellite systems — university satellites doing the hardware qualification work that makes future missions reliable.

42831.d: Named for its laptop-like form factor, this German small satellite was built by students at the University of Stuttgart. It carries a multi-spectral Earth imager and tests new on-board computing systems for the harsh radiation environment of low Earth orbit. Flying Laptop represents Germany's university tradition of producing flight-heritage technology that feeds directly into commercial and scientific missions.

42907.d: A classified Russian military satellite in geostationary orbit. Russia routinely launches highly capable military satellites under the Cosmos designation, which provides a level of ambiguity about their missions. COSMOS 2520's orbital position is monitored by independent tracking organizations, but its specific capabilities remain undisclosed. Russia's classified GEO presence mirrors the US approach of maintaining unacknowledged national security payloads in the strategic geostationary belt.

42915.d: The most recent addition to NASA's space communications backbone. TDRS-13 provides continuous relay capability for human spaceflight and robotic science missions from a fixed geostationary position. As the ISS approaches retirement, TDRS will become critical for supporting the commercial space stations planned to replace it, and TDRS-13 will be among the newest assets supporting that transition.

42917.d: The geostationary component of Japan's Michibiki constellation — parked permanently at 127°E over the Pacific. QZS-3 provides constant GPS augmentation signals over Japan without the orbital motion of the quasi-zenith satellites, providing redundancy and ensuring that Japan always has at least one Michibiki satellite nearly overhead. It also carries experimental Ka-band inter-satellite links and a Search and Rescue payload to relay distress signals from maritime emergencies.

42920.d: Taiwan's first independently developed Earth observation satellite, deliberately built with 70% domestic content to grow Taiwan's space engineering capability. Its true value was in the engineers it trained — who built Taiwan's next-generation FORMOSAT-7 constellation, establishing Taiwan as a serious space technology nation.

42934.d: Hispasat's most powerful Americas satellite, incorporating high-throughput Ka-band payload alongside traditional Ku-band capacity. Amazonas-5 provides the backbone for Brazilian satellite internet services targeting the country's rural connectivity gap — 30 million Brazilians lack any broadband access, most in the Amazon region. The satellite enables Brazil's government programs aiming to connect every school and health post across the country's vast interior.

42951.d: Japan's newest direct-to-home broadcasting satellite at 110°E, providing the enhanced power and capacity needed for Japan's nationwide 4K Ultra HD broadcasting service. BSAT-4A delivers 4K content from NHK and commercial broadcasters to home viewers using consumer satellite receivers — part of Japan's nationally coordinated digital broadcasting upgrade that aims to make 4K the standard for Japanese home television by 2025.

42965.d: The fourth Michibiki satellite, completing Japan's initial quasi-zenith constellation. With four satellites, Japan achieved 24-hour continuous high-elevation coverage — ensuring that at any moment, Japanese users have a Michibiki satellite nearly overhead that can provide sub-centimeter positioning corrections. This enabled Japan to begin deploying centimeter-accurate autonomous agricultural machinery across its rice paddies, reducing labor requirements in Japan's aging farming sector.

42969.d: Europe's atmospheric chemistry sentinel maps air pollution globally every single day at 3.5 km resolution. Its TROPOMI instrument measures nitrogen dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ozone, methane, and carbon monoxide simultaneously. Within hours of a factory explosion or wildfire, Sentinel-5P shows the toxic plume drifting across continents — pollution made visible from orbit in near real-time.

42984.d: South Korea's military communications satellite, providing secure Ku-band connectivity for Korean armed forces including the army, navy, air force, and Marine Corps. KoreaSat-5A combines commercial services with military-reserved capacity, providing Korea's defense establishment with protected satellite communications — essential for a military facing the most intensely militarized border in the world and operating under constant threat of North Korean electronic warfare.
42984.a: KT SAT / Republic of Korea Military

42987.d: SkySat-C11, part of Planet's mature operational constellation. By this point Planet had transformed from a startup to the world's largest commercial Earth observation operator — capturing more images of Earth per day than all previous commercial operators combined.

42988.d: The tenth SkySat satellite. Planet's open data approach for lower-resolution imagery, combined with its commercial SkySat service, established a model for Earth observation that has been copied by dozens of competitors worldwide — democratizing space-based imagery in ways previously unimaginable.

42989.d: SkySat-C9 is part of the constellation that made Planet Labs the most prolific satellite operator in history. Planet's strategy — many cheap satellites instead of few expensive ones — proved that Moore's Law could be applied to Earth observation, making space-based imagery cheaper and more accessible every year.

42990.d: SkySat-C8 contributed to Planet achieving the milestone of daily global coverage at 72 cm resolution, making the company the world's first commercial operator to offer that capability. This marked the moment satellite imagery transitioned from a specialty product to a broadly accessible commercial service.

42991.d: Part of Planet's rapid 2017 expansion. The SkySat satellites offer both still imagery and 90-second video clips — operators choose the mode most useful for their application. For tracking moving vessels, vehicles, or crowds, video from orbit provides information no number of still images can replicate.

42992.d: SkySat-C6, part of a six-satellite batch that dramatically accelerated Planet's coverage. With 12+ SkySats in orbit, Planet achieved near-global daily coverage at 72 cm — the threshold where its constellation became a genuine alternative to traditional government reconnaissance programs for many applications.

43010.d: FY-3D introduced China's first space-based hyperspectral greenhouse gas monitor, measuring CO2 and methane from orbit. Its atmospheric soundings feed China's rapidly improving global weather models — now matching European and American models in accuracy over China and neighboring regions, a remarkable advance achieved in under a decade.

43013.d: The first Joint Polar Satellite System satellite collects more data per orbit than any previous NOAA weather satellite. Its VIIRS instrument maps global land surface, sea ice, ocean color, and fire locations every day — the complete surface picture that feeds the 7-day forecasts you check on your phone.

43039.d: Algeria's first national communications satellite, a milestone for North African space capabilities. AlComSat-1 provides direct-to-home TV broadcasting, broadband internet, and government communications across Algeria — a country the size of Western Europe where terrestrial infrastructure struggles to reach remote Saharan regions. The satellite was built by China Great Wall Industry Corporation and represents Algeria's growing space partnership with China.

43111.d: Cartosat-2F represents India's reliable high-resolution imaging workhorse. The series' quality and reliability helped establish ISRO's credibility as a world-class Earth observation manufacturer — contributing to India's growing portfolio of satellite technology exports to developing nations seeking affordable Earth observation capability.

43162.d: The third SBIRS satellite, adding capacity to the missile warning constellation. SBIRS GEO-3 provides theater missile defense support beyond its strategic nuclear warning role — detecting shorter-range ballistic missiles launched in conflict zones with enough accuracy to cue Patriot batteries, THAAD systems, and naval interceptors before impact. This theater tracking capability was not part of the original SBIRS design but emerged as a critical operational need.

43174.d: Yahsat's high-throughput Ka-band satellite focused on West Africa and Brazil — two of the world's fastest-growing satellite internet markets. Al Yah 3 uses 60 Ka-band spot beams to concentrate capacity where demand is highest, enabling Yahsat to offer residential and enterprise broadband at competitive prices across regions where terrestrial alternatives are years from reaching most of the population. Al Yah means 'God' in Arabic — Yahsat's name literally means 'God's Satellite.'

43194.d: Named after a 2nd-century Chinese polymath who invented the world's first seismoscope, Zhangheng-1 searches for electromagnetic disturbances in the ionosphere before earthquakes. The controversial theory holds that seismic activity creates detectable changes in the ionosphere hours before a quake. Whether or not that link proves out, the satellite has built an invaluable baseline dataset of the ionosphere's normal electromagnetic behavior — useful for space weather, communications, and navigation science.

43226.d: NOAA's GOES-West satellite covering the Pacific Ocean and western United States. GOES-17 watches California wildfires, Pacific typhoons, and Alaskan weather with the same advanced 16-band imager as GOES-16. Its lightning mapper tracks electrical storms crossing from the Pacific that become California's atmospheric rivers dumping feet of rain on the mountains.

43228.d: Hispasat's next-generation Americas satellite providing Ka-band high-throughput broadband alongside traditional Ku-band broadcasting capacity. Hispasat 30W-6 serves the growing Latin American demand for satellite internet as terrestrial infrastructure fails to reach the full population. Spain's national investment in satellite communications reflects both commercial opportunity and the soft-power benefit of providing connectivity infrastructure to Spanish-speaking nations across the hemisphere.

43259.d: One of three identical Gaofen-1 follow-on satellites achieving 2-day global coverage at 2-meter resolution — fast enough for operational disaster monitoring across China and neighboring countries during active typhoon impacts, earthquake aftermaths, and flood events.

43260.d: The second of three Gaofen-1 follow-on satellites. China's strategy of deploying multiple identical satellites in constellation — accepting slightly lower capability per satellite for dramatically higher coverage frequency — mirrors the Copernicus approach that transformed European Earth observation.

43262.d: The third Gaofen-1 follow-on completing the triplet, giving China's disaster reduction agencies near-real-time satellite coverage during active emergencies. A capability previously available only to the US, Europe, and Japan — now fielded by China at full operational scale.

43271.d: A Japanese commercial satellite providing Ku-band broadcasting and communications across Japan and the Asia-Pacific. SuperBird-B3 serves Japan's satellite television market — home to some of the world's highest-definition broadcasting — and supports Japanese maritime communications for the world's third-largest merchant fleet. Japan's satellite operators have built sophisticated service portfolios reflecting the country's advanced digital economy requirements.

43272.d: Avanti's third-generation Ka-band high-throughput satellite, significantly expanding European broadband coverage. HYLAS-4 provides broadband connectivity to rural European communities that fall below the threshold for commercial fiber or 5G deployment — a significant population across Eastern Europe, the Balkans, and the Mediterranean. The satellite also supports maritime connectivity for European fishing and commercial fleets across the Atlantic and Mediterranean.

43286.d: A NavIC replacement satellite, launched after three of the original IRNSS atomic clocks failed on orbit — a crisis that threatened the accuracy of India's navigation system. IRNSS-1I was rapidly designed, built, and launched to restore full constellation capability, demonstrating ISRO's ability to respond quickly to on-orbit anomalies. It carries improved rubidium atomic clocks designed to avoid the fault modes that affected earlier satellites.

43339.d: A classified US military satellite launched in 2018 into geostationary orbit. Its mission is unacknowledged, but its position and behavior are monitored by independent orbital analysts. The US Space Force regularly places classified payloads in GEO that support both intelligence gathering and assured communications for the most sensitive national security missions.

43432.d: A Russian classified satellite in geostationary orbit assessed as a military communications payload. Russia's military space communications architecture depends heavily on GEO assets to maintain command links across its 11 time zones and to coordinate its strategic nuclear forces. The anonymous Cosmos designation provides operational security by obscuring the satellite's specific mission and capabilities.

43437.d: Flying offset from Sentinel-3A by half an orbit, Sentinel-3B doubles ocean and land monitoring coverage. Together the pair provides the most complete operational picture of global sea surface temperature and ocean color ever achieved — data feeding fisheries management, shipping, and climate models serving hundreds of millions of people.

43450.d: A high-power C-band Chinese commercial satellite serving Southeast Asian broadcasting markets. APStar-6C provides the high-power C-band signals needed for direct-to-home TV reception in tropical Southeast Asia — where C-band's resistance to heavy rainfall fading makes it essential for reliable broadcasting across countries like Indonesia, the Philippines, and Papua New Guinea where frequent intense rain would disrupt Ku-band signals.

43461.d: China's first hyperspectral satellite measures 330 spectral bands simultaneously — splitting light into 330 colors to identify mineral composition at the surface, detect pollutants in water bodies, and distinguish crop species that look identical in standard imagery. Hyperspectral vision from orbit: seeing what the eye cannot.

43463.d: Bangladesh's first satellite, named after Bangabandhu — 'Friend of Bengal' — the honorific given to Sheikh Mujibur Rahman, the founding father of Bangladesh who led the country's independence movement. Bangabandhu Satellite-1 provides direct-to-home TV broadcasting, internet connectivity, and disaster warning communications across Bangladesh and surrounding regions. For a low-lying country extremely vulnerable to cyclones and floods, reliable communications satellite capacity is not just commercial — it's a matter of national survival.

43484.d: Gaofen-6 adds a red-edge spectral band sensitive to chlorophyll — detecting crop stress weeks before visible wilting. Combined with Gaofen-1, it feeds China's national crop condition monitoring system guiding food security policy and export decisions for the world's largest agricultural nation.

43491.d: Deployed at 79°E to cover the Indian Ocean, FY-2H monitors weather systems in the Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal that breed cyclones threatening India's coasts. It shares data with the World Meteorological Organization as part of China's growing role in global weather data infrastructure for neighboring nations.

43539.d: One of the last BeiDou-2 IGSO satellites, maintaining the regional navigation service as BeiDou-3 takes over global navigation. The BeiDou-2 IGSO component provides continuity for millions of devices designed for the earlier system, ensuring backward compatibility as China transitions to its globally capable third-generation constellation.

43585.d: China's highest-resolution optical intelligence satellite, assessed as delivering sub-50 cm imagery comparable to US KH-11 reconnaissance satellites. Its deployment marked China's achievement of near-parity with the United States in space-based optical intelligence gathering — one of the most strategically significant space developments of the decade.

43613.d: NASA's ICESat-2 fires 10,000 laser pulses per second and times returning photons with picosecond precision — measuring ice sheet height to within 1 centimeter. Its measurements revealed Greenland's ice sheet is losing mass 6 times faster than in the 1990s. One laser beam aimed at melting ice, producing numbers that reshape our understanding of sea level rise.

43632.d: Azerbaijan's second national satellite, operated through a partnership with Intelsat that provides additional capacity and frequency resources. AzerSpace-2 extends Azercosmos's coverage to Europe and Africa, positioning Azerbaijan's satellite operator as a regional player in a competitive market. The satellite provides DTH broadcasting, broadband, and corporate network services, supported by Azerbaijan's strategic position at the crossroads of Europe and Asia.

43651.d: The fourth AEHF satellite, providing redundancy and additional capacity to the protected communications constellation. AEHF-4 significantly expands the number of terminals the constellation can support simultaneously, enabling more units at every level of command to access protected links. As military communications evolved from voice-only to data-intensive over IP networks, AEHF-4 ensures those data links are as protected as the original nuclear command channels.

43683.d: The first geostationary satellite of the global BeiDou-3 navigation system — China's complete answer to GPS with worldwide coverage. BeiDou-3 GEO satellites extend the Short Message Service to a global audience and provide high-power navigation signals over the Asia-Pacific. China declared BeiDou-3 fully operational in 2020, achieving independence from GPS for Chinese military navigation globally — one of the most strategically significant space capabilities China has ever developed.

43689.d: The third MetOp completes Europe's polar weather trio. MetOp-C and MetOp-B together revisit every point on Earth four times daily. Its ASCAT scatterometer measures wind speed over the entire global ocean — essential for detecting rogue waves and safely routing shipping through the Southern Ocean's legendary storms.

43698.d: A multiband communications satellite serving northeastern India and rural regions with Ka and Ku-band connectivity. GSAT-29 specifically targets India's most connectivity-deprived regions — Jammu and Kashmir, the Northeast states, and island territories — where terrestrial infrastructure is constrained by terrain, security considerations, or geographic isolation. Its high-power Ka-band spots concentrate capacity exactly where India's government programs need it most.

43700.d: Qatar's second satellite — famous among radio amateurs worldwide for carrying the first geostationary amateur radio transponder in history, known as QO-100. QO-100's 36 MHz wide transponder allows amateur radio operators from Brazil to India to conduct two-way conversations through the satellite using modest equipment — a historic milestone for global amateur radio, now permanently accessible from the geostationary arc. Es'hail-2 also provides Qatar's commercial Ku and Ka-band communications services.

43719.d: India's first hyperspectral satellite measures 55 bands in visible light and 256 bands in shortwave infrared — enough spectral resolution to identify mineral deposits, assess crop health across India's 180 million agricultural plots, and monitor invasive species spreading across India's diverse ecosystems.

43765.d: The Pathfinder cluster of HawkEye 360's constellation — three satellites flying in precise formation to geolocate radio frequency signals anywhere on Earth using triangulation. By comparing the time each satellite receives the same signal, the cluster pinpoints any radio transmitter to within hundreds of meters. Applications range from tracking ships disabling their AIS transponders to monitoring unauthorized radio emissions across conflict zones.

43783.d: Kazakhstan's satellite provides imagery for Central Asian challenges: the shrinking Aral Sea, steppe desertification, and the vast wheat belt. Kazakhstan's territory is too large for any ground-based environmental survey — satellite observation isn't a supplement but the primary means of understanding the country's land and resources.

43794.d: The second satellite in HawkEye 360's Pathfinder formation. The three-satellite formation must maintain extremely precise relative positions for accurate triangulation — each satellite's position known to within meters for the time-difference-of-arrival calculation to yield useful geolocation. This formation-flying precision was the key technology that made commercial RF geolocation from orbit viable.

43799.d: The third Pathfinder satellite, completing the trio that first proved commercial space-based RF geolocation. When all three detect the same radio signal, the 10-millisecond differences in arrival time combined with known satellite positions reveal the transmitter's location anywhere on Earth. The Pathfinder proved this commercially, leading to rapid constellation expansion.

43823.d: South Korea's first advanced geostationary weather satellite, GK-2A delivers 16-channel full-disk imagery every 10 minutes — sharp enough to watch individual thunderstorm cells boil up in real time. Korea developed it largely with domestic technology, marking its emergence as a tier-one geostationary weather satellite nation alongside the US, Japan, and Europe.

43824.d: India's most powerful communications satellite — a 5.8-ton spacecraft with 40 Gbps Ka-band throughput, ISRO's first high-throughput satellite. GSAT-11 supports India's ambitious broadband connectivity programs extending satellite internet to rural India's schools, hospitals, and government services. With 32 user beams and 8 gateway beams, it provides enough capacity to serve millions of broadband subscribers across the subcontinent.

43831.d: Saudi Arabia's Earth observation satellite contributes to the Kingdom's growing sovereign space capability. As Saudi Arabia pursues Vision 2030 goals of space technology independence, each domestic satellite builds the engineering ecosystem needed to eventually produce advanced systems entirely within the Kingdom.

43833.d: The companion to SaudiSat-5A, together they provide Saudi Arabia with stereo imaging capability — capturing overlapping views to generate 3D terrain models of the Arabian Peninsula and surrounding region, supporting the Kingdom's infrastructure development and resource management programs.

43864.d: India's second dedicated military communications satellite, this one serving the Indian Air Force. GSAT-7A provides connectivity for Indian Air Force aircraft, ground-based defense networks, and drone operations — enabling network-centric warfare capabilities for India's growing fleet of advanced combat aircraft. Like its naval predecessor GSAT-7, it was built on ISRO's I-3000 satellite bus and represents India's growing military space capabilities.

43867.d: A classified Russian military satellite in geostationary orbit. The Cosmos numbering system has been used since 1962 to obscure the missions of Russian military spacecraft — a tradition spanning the entire Space Age. COSMOS 2533 is monitored by amateur satellite trackers who share orbital data, providing transparency about the object's existence and position even when its mission remains officially secret.

43874.d: One of the most observed TJS satellites — in November 2018, a classified US satellite (USA-276, believed to be a Geosynchronous Space Situational Awareness Program spacecraft) maneuvered within 6 km of TJS-3 and maintained proximity for several months. The US satellite appeared to inspect TJS-3 at close range. This represented the first publicly observed instance of one nation's satellite shadowing another's in geostationary orbit — and raised international concerns about the militarization of GEO.

43920.d: One of China's classified military communications satellites in the Zhongxing series. Zhongxing-2D provides secure military communications for the People's Liberation Army, supporting China's strategic nuclear forces, naval operations, and command networks across China's territory and global military presence. The 2D designation reflects China's ongoing modernization of its military satellite communications architecture.

44034.d: A dual-payload satellite carrying both a Hellas-Sat commercial communications payload and Saudi Geostationary Satellite-1 (SGS-1) for Saudi Arabia. This co-hosted configuration — two satellite operators sharing a single spacecraft — reduces launch costs and makes use of unused capacity on the satellite bus. SGS-1 provides Saudi government and military communications while Hellas-Sat 4 extends Greek commercial satellite coverage to the Middle East and Indian Ocean.

44035.d: An Indian communications satellite extending Ku-band capacity across the Indian Ocean region, supporting maritime communications, VSAT networks, and government services across South Asia. GSAT-31 provides continuity of service for Indian customers as older satellites reach end of life — an increasingly important role as ISRO builds operational satellite maintenance capability alongside its launch and spacecraft development programs.

44048.d: A commercial Indonesian communications satellite built by SSL (now Maxar) incorporating one of the first high-throughput Ka-band payloads over Southeast Asia. Nusantara Satu serves Indonesia's enormous connectivity gap — the world's largest archipelago with 17,000+ islands and a population of 270 million, where terrestrial connectivity serves only major urban areas. It hosted a secondary payload for SpaceIL's Beresheet lunar lander mission, making it part of a unique dual mission.

44067.d: A Chinese C-band communications satellite providing extensive coverage across the Asia-Pacific and the Middle East. C-band satellites are particularly valuable for professional video distribution and government communications in tropical regions where rain fade affects higher-frequency Ku-band signals — making Zhongxing-6C useful for Chinese broadcasting and government communications across Southeast Asia and the broader Indo-Pacific.

44071.d: The tenth WGS satellite, completing the planned Wideband Global SATCOM constellation. Together the ten WGS satellites provide the US and its allies with more military broadband satellite capacity than the rest of the world combined. The constellation has transformed military operations — drones can stream 4K video, submarines can receive intelligence updates, and special operations forces can access classified databases from virtually anywhere on Earth.

44076.d: The first second-generation Tianlian satellite, providing dramatically higher data rates than Tianlian-1 for China's increasingly ambitious human spaceflight program. Tianlian-2 supports the full bandwidth requirements of the Tiangong Space Station — multiple simultaneous video streams, large science data downloads, and continuous command uplink — as China ramps up both crewed missions and station utilization.

44186.d: The most powerful Arab world communications satellite ever launched, incorporating 60 Ku-band, 6 Ka-band, and 4 C-band transponders on a Boeing 702 platform. Arabsat-6A serves the full range of Arab broadcasting and broadband requirements across the Middle East and Africa, providing the capacity backbone for some of the Arab world's most-watched satellite TV platforms including beIN Sports and MBC.

44204.d: The first BeiDou-3 inclined geosynchronous orbit satellite, bringing next-generation navigation signals to the IGSO component. BeiDou-3 IGSO satellites provide the B1C signal compatible with GPS L1C, enabling receiver manufacturers to build single chips that work with both BeiDou and GPS — dramatically lowering the cost barrier for adopting BeiDou and explaining its rapid international uptake.

44231.d: One of the last BeiDou-2 GEO satellites, maintaining the regional navigation network as China transitions to BeiDou-3's global capability. BeiDou-2's GEO satellites continue to serve Chinese users while BeiDou-3's MEO and IGSO satellites provide global positioning — the two systems operate in parallel, with GEO providing the strongest local signals and MEO/IGSO enabling worldwide coverage.

44307.d: Russia's most advanced commercial communications satellite, featuring a high-throughput Ka-band payload providing broadband internet across Russia. Yamal-601 addressed Russia's strategic goal of reducing dependence on foreign satellite capacity — much of Russia's broadband satellite internet had previously been sourced through foreign commercial operators. It represents Russia's commitment to domestic communications sovereignty alongside its military space programs.

44337.d: The second BeiDou-3 IGSO satellite, completing the initial IGSO component of the global BeiDou-3 constellation. BeiDou-3 IGSO satellites are more powerful than BeiDou-2, providing stronger signals in urban environments and in the mountainous terrain of Central and South Asia where BeiDou has made the deepest international inroads. The combination of higher power and multi-frequency signals makes BeiDou-3 competitive with the best GPS performance available.

44387.d: Russia's second Meteor-M No.2 satellite fills polar weather coverage gaps over Siberia and the Arctic. It provides imagery sharp enough to spot sea ice leads — narrow channels of open water that are navigation lifelines for Arctic shipping as Russia develops the Northern Sea Route.

44457.d: A classified Russian military satellite in geostationary orbit. Its launch and orbital position align with the timing and placement of Russian military communications satellite replacements, suggesting a communications or data relay role. Russia has accelerated its military space program significantly in recent years, launching new GEO satellites at a higher rate than at any time since the Soviet era.

44475.d: The European Data Relay System's second node — a space-based laser communication relay that connects Earth observation satellites to ground stations across Europe at 1.8 Gbps via optical link. Instead of waiting for a satellite to pass over a ground station, EDRS-C continuously receives laser beams from LEO satellites and relays data to ground via Ka-band radio. It serves Sentinel Earth observation satellites, receiving their data continuously rather than in bursts — dramatically reducing the time from observation to data delivery for European Copernicus emergency response operations.

44481.d: The fifth AEHF satellite, launched on the first national security mission for a SpaceX Falcon 9 — a milestone marking the end of United Launch Alliance's monopoly on US military satellite launches. AEHF-5 provides additional coverage and capacity, particularly for users in high latitudes including Alaska and the Arctic, where military activity has increased as ice retreat opens new strategic domains.

44622.d: A radar reconnaissance satellite in China's Gaofen constellation providing all-weather imaging to complement optical variants. SAR capability eliminates the cloud-cover vulnerability that affects optical satellites over much of monsoon-dominated Asia — ensuring China's Earth observation capability is truly persistent regardless of weather.

44625.d: The spacecraft that proved satellite servicing works commercially. Northrop Grumman's Mission Extension Vehicle-1 made history in February 2020 as the first spacecraft ever to dock with another commercial satellite in geostationary orbit — latching onto Intelsat 901 to take over its propulsion and give the ailing spacecraft five additional years of life. MEV-1 docked in the graveyard orbit above GEO, then flew the combined stack back into operational position. It successfully completed that five-year extension in April 2025, then undocked and moved on to its next customer — proving the entire satellite servicing business model. A spacecraft that spent 35 years in orbit is now being decommissioned, while MEV-1 flies on to serve again.

44637.d: A TJS satellite that has displayed unusual sub-satellite deployment behavior — releasing smaller objects into nearby orbits during its operational life. This capability could represent testing of inspection, jamming, or proximity operations technologies, or it may relate to hosted payload deployment for intelligence collection. China has not explained these maneuvers, which have been closely tracked by US Space Force and independent analysts.

44800.d: Egypt's first government satellite, providing C and Ku-band communications for Egyptian government agencies, defense establishments, and public service applications. TIBA-1 (Technological Innovation Broadband for Africa) represents Egypt's investment in sovereign satellite communications as the country positions itself as a potential satellite services hub for Africa. The satellite carries both civilian government and military communications capacity.

44801.d: The fifth Global Xpress satellite, with focused spot beam coverage over Europe and the North Atlantic for maximum capacity where aviation and maritime traffic is densest. GX5 uses a high-throughput spot beam architecture that concentrates Ka-band power precisely where demand is highest — major shipping lanes, European airspace, and densely traveled North Atlantic flight corridors — rather than spreading it uniformly across a wide coverage area.

44804.d: India's sharpest satellite achieves 25 cm resolution — comparable to US commercial leaders WorldView and Pleiades. Cartosat-3 represents India's arrival at the elite tier of very high resolution imaging, supporting defense mapping, urban planning, and disaster response at a level previously requiring foreign commercial imagery purchases.

44819.d: China's first Gaofen-12 high-resolution SAR satellite delivers sub-meter radar imagery day and night in all weather. The series has expanded rapidly, providing China with unprecedented persistent radar surveillance of global maritime and land targets — the all-conditions coverage that is the holy grail of satellite intelligence.

44868.d: A dual-purpose satellite operated by Sky Perfect JSAT and Kacific Broadband Satellites, specifically designed to bring Ka-band broadband to Pacific Island nations — among the world's most digitally isolated communities. Kacific-1 serves 25 Pacific nations from Micronesia to Samoa with affordable satellite broadband, connecting island communities to education, healthcare, and economic opportunity that was previously inaccessible due to the Pacific Ocean's vast distances and lack of undersea cable infrastructure.

44873.d: COSMO-SkyMed Second Generation launches Italy's next chapter in radar Earth observation. CSG-1 can image in spotlight mode at 40 cm resolution — among the world's sharpest radar satellites. Italy operates one of the most sophisticated dual-use Earth observation systems in the world, serving both scientific and national security missions from the same constellation.

44910.d: One of the largest satellites ever launched by China — over 8 metric tons — Shijian-20 tested high-power electric propulsion, laser communications, and other technologies intended for China's next generation of large geostationary satellites. Its massive solar arrays and advanced propulsion system demonstrated technologies China plans to incorporate into future military and commercial GEO platforms requiring high power and orbital flexibility.

44978.d: The fifth TJS satellite, part of China's rapidly expanding classified GEO military presence. TJS-5's orbital position provides coverage complementary to earlier TJS satellites, suggesting the series is building a global military communications network rather than merely conducting isolated technology experiments. China's pace of GEO military satellite launches has accelerated significantly since 2018, reflecting the growing strategic importance China places on the space domain.

45026.d: India's C and Ku-band commercial communications satellite, providing a full range of broadcasting and broadband services across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and Australia. GSAT-30 replaced aging capacity and added new service zones, expanding India's commercial satellite reach beyond the subcontinent. It serves direct-to-home TV broadcasting for India's enormous television market — one of the world's largest with over 800 satellite TV channels.

45245.d: Sky Perfect JSAT's commercial satellite expanding capacity for Japan's growing broadband satellite market. JCSAT-17 provides Ka-band and Ku-band services for both Japanese domestic customers and regional Asia-Pacific markets. Japan's commercial satellite operators have benefited from the country's technology leadership while facing growing competition from global operators and low-Earth orbit broadband constellations.

45344.d: The second BeiDou-3 geostationary satellite, providing redundancy and enhanced short message service capacity for the global BeiDou constellation. BeiDou-3 GEO satellites are also part of China's satellite-based augmentation system, providing high-precision positioning corrections that enable centimeter-level accuracy across the Asia-Pacific — critical for Chinese precision-guided weapons and autonomous systems.

45465.d: The sixth and final planned AEHF satellite, completing the most capable protected military communications architecture ever deployed. AEHF-6 provides the capacity and redundancy needed to support the full spectrum of US military global operations. Together, the six AEHF satellites have permanently replaced MILSTAR as America's nuclear and conventional protected communications backbone — a system that will define US military command and control for decades.

45807.d: The third BeiDou-3 geostationary navigation satellite, completing the core GEO component of China's global navigation system. With three GEO satellites operational, BeiDou-3 achieves its design coverage for enhanced signal strength across the Asia-Pacific while maintaining worldwide positioning from its MEO satellites. China now operates the world's most capable regional navigation system alongside one of its global counterparts.

45863.d: APStar's Ka-band high-throughput satellite providing broadband connectivity across China and the Asia-Pacific. APStar-6D targets China's maritime broadband market — providing internet to fishing fleets, cargo vessels, and coastal monitoring stations across the South China Sea and broader Pacific. China's extensive maritime interests across contested and contested-adjacent waters make reliable satellite communications for vessels a strategic priority.

45920.d: A Korean military communications satellite providing secure government and defense connectivity. South Korea has invested in dedicated military satellite capacity separate from its commercial operators — recognizing that a country facing the North Korean threat requires guaranteed, hardened military communications that don't share capacity with commercial users who might face outages during crises.

45985.d: A new-generation Russian communications satellite providing capacity across Russia's European territory, Central Asia, and neighboring regions. Express-103 carries advanced payloads for broadband internet delivery and government communications, representing Russia's continued investment in sovereign satellite communications infrastructure amid Western sanctions that have complicated access to foreign satellite technology.

45986.d: Launched alongside Express-103 on a single Proton rocket, Express-80 covers Russia's core market of European Russia and neighboring countries. Together the Express-80 and 103 pair significantly expanded Russian commercial satellite capacity at a time when Russia faced growing pressure from global commercial satellite operators. Russia's satellite communications sector remains largely insulated from Western competition over its domestic market.

46112.d: The companion to BSAT-4A, completing Japan's high-definition broadcasting satellite pair. BSAT-4B provides redundancy and additional capacity for Japan's 4K and experimental 8K broadcasting — NHK has been conducting 8K (Super Hi-Vision) satellite broadcasts using BSAT capacity, transmitting the world's highest-resolution satellite television to viewers in Japan at 120 frames per second with 22.2-channel audio. Japan's public broadcaster treats 8K satellite broadcasting as a national technology showcase.

46113.d: The second Mission Extension Vehicle, currently docked to Intelsat 10-02 and extended its service contract to provide four additional years of life. MEV-2 docked while IS-10-02 was still in its operational GEO slot — a more challenging approach than MEV-1 used — demonstrating mature satellite servicing capability without interrupting service to the 18 million European TV households the satellite serves. MEV-2 represents the beginning of a commercial ecosystem where satellite operators routinely budget for in-orbit servicing rather than replacement.

46277.d: Slovenia's first satellite, built by startup SatLab, produces video from orbit at 2.8 meter resolution from a 65 kg nanosatellite — proving professional-quality Earth video could be achieved on a university budget. Small nations discovering they can reach orbit is one of the defining stories of the current era of spaceflight.

46495.d: A German nanosatellite from TU Berlin testing attitude control using only magnetic torquers — no reaction wheels, no moving parts. This ultra-simple approach could make nanosatellites dramatically more reliable and longer-lived. At barely 2 kg and roughly the size of a hardback book, SALSAT demonstrates how much capability can be packed into the smallest satellites.

46610.d: China's second high-resolution geostationary imaging satellite, following Gaofen-4. Unlike polar-orbiting satellites that revisit a location once per day, Gaofen-13 can continuously stare at a target for hours — tracking weather systems, disaster development, or military activity in real time from 36,000 km altitude. At 0.5-meter optical resolution from geostationary orbit, it represents an extraordinary technical achievement — equivalent to reading a license plate from the height of 35,800 km.

46984.d: Named Michael Freilich in honor of the late NASA oceanographer, Sentinel-6A is the most accurate sea level measurement satellite ever operated. It revealed that global sea level rise accelerated significantly between 2014 and 2020 — a critical finding for infrastructure planning in every coastal city on Earth. The data that changes how civilization plans its future coastlines.

47202.d: Japan's first optical inter-orbit communication relay satellite — the Japanese equivalent of Europe's EDRS-C. LUCAS (Laser Utilizing Communication System) uses laser links to relay data from Japanese government satellites at gigabit rates, feeding observation data to ground stations in near-real-time regardless of the source satellite's location. Japan designed LUCAS to support its ALOS-4 radar satellite and future intelligence satellites, dramatically improving the timeliness of imagery intelligence for Japanese defense applications.

47240.d: The first of SiriusXM's next-generation satellite radio spacecraft, built to replace aging earlier satellites. SXM-7 provides the full SiriusXM channel lineup — over 150 channels of commercial-free music, news, sports, and entertainment — to subscribers in vehicles, homes, and on mobile apps. Following its December 2020 launch, it suffered a partial power failure but remained usable. SiriusXM's GEO satellite architecture allows a single car satellite radio antenna to receive programming across the entire North American continent.

47256.d: An Indian commercial communications satellite providing extended C-band coverage for South Asian customers. CMS-01 replaced the aging GSAT-14 satellite, maintaining continuity of the Ku-band capacity that Indian television broadcasters and corporate network operators depend on. It covers the Indian subcontinent, Bay of Bengal, and Arabian Sea — the geographic range of India's commercial satellite connectivity requirements.

47306.d: Turkey's first all-electric satellite — a major step in Turkey's space technology ambitions. Turksat-5A provides high-throughput Ku-band capacity specifically for Turkey's growing satellite broadband market, extending internet access to rural Anatolian regions where terrestrial broadband is limited. Turkey's Turksat operator is expanding from a domestic broadcaster into a regional satellite internet provider, competing for customers across the Middle East and Central Asia.

47502.d: The third Cluster 2 satellite, completing the second operational HawkEye 360 formation. With two clusters operational, HawkEye 360 deployed its first maritime domain awareness commercial product — locating 'dark ships' hiding illicit cargo movements from legal AIS tracking systems.

47505.d: First satellite of HawkEye 360's Cluster 2, transitioning from pathfinder demo to operational RF geolocation service. Cluster 2 incorporated lessons from the Pathfinder — improved formation flying, enhanced signal processing, and extended frequency coverage — making commercial RF geolocation a reliable product for government and commercial customers.

47508.d: The second satellite of HawkEye 360 Cluster 2, operational in the growing RF geolocation constellation. Primary commercial applications include maritime domain awareness — detecting vessels that have switched off their AIS transponders to hide movements — and monitoring radio frequency interference affecting GPS and communications satellites.

47613.d: A TJS satellite observed releasing a sub-satellite into GEO orbit — one of a series of Chinese GEO satellites that have deployed smaller companion objects. These deployment events, combined with the classified nature of the TJS series, have raised concerns among US Space Force analysts about potential anti-satellite capabilities being developed and tested in the geostationary belt. TJS-6's activities have been tracked by multiple independent orbital analysts.

47719.d: Russia's revolutionary Arctic weather satellite in a highly elliptical Molniya orbit — spending most of its time over Russia's north rather than flying over the equator like geostationary satellites. Geostationary birds see the Arctic from a shallow angle; Arktika-M looks straight down, providing the first geostationary-equivalent weather imagery of the North Pole.

47932.d: China's 500 kg commercial observation platform demonstrates CAST's modular satellite bus supporting high-resolution payloads at dramatically lower cost than traditional systems. Part of China's pivot toward commercial satellite services that can compete globally — following the path of Chinese technology manufacturers who moved from domestic supply to world export.

48079.d: The second Gaofen-12 SAR satellite adds capacity to China's all-weather radar surveillance constellation. With multiple satellites in orbit, China can achieve same-day radar imaging of any global target — eliminating the temporal gap that previously forced analysts to wait hours or days for a satellite to pass overhead.

48268.d: Airbus's sharpest commercial satellites yet, Pleiades Neo 3 achieves 30 cm native resolution — the finest commercially available optical imagery from space. At this resolution individual people can be distinguished from their shadows. With its twin, it can revisit any point on Earth up to 10 times daily. The boundary between commercial and intelligence-grade imaging has effectively ceased to exist.

48618.d: The fifth SBIRS satellite, built to an enhanced design with improved sensitivity and a new missile defense support processor. SBIRS GEO-5 was the first to use a modernized Long Wave Infrared (LWIR) detector array that dramatically improves tracking accuracy for hypersonic glide vehicles — the new class of maneuvering warhead that traditional missile warning systems struggle to track through their unpredictable flight paths.

48838.d: SiriusXM's companion next-generation satellite, completing the pair that ensures uninterrupted satellite radio service across North America. SXM-8 provides the geographic diversity that protects SiriusXM from service outages — with two satellites in slightly different geostationary positions, subscribers are always in range of at least one even in parking garages, urban canyons, and hilly terrain where one satellite's signal might be blocked.

48931.d: The second satellite of HawkEye 360 Cluster 3. As the constellation has grown, customers have expanded from maritime awareness to monitoring GPS jammers — a growing problem as militaries and criminal organizations deploy jamming that disrupts civilian aviation, shipping, and emergency services.

48948.d: The third satellite of Cluster 3. The full HawkEye 360 system has geolocated illegal radio transmitters in dozens of countries, detected vessels transporting sanctioned oil, and helped the US Coast Guard identify suspicious fishing vessels — converting satellite data directly into law enforcement and national security value.

48957.d: First satellite of HawkEye 360's third cluster. Each new cluster reduces the average revisit time over any point on Earth, improving timeliness of detecting suspicious radio emissions or tracking vessels attempting to transit undetected through strategic choke points like the Strait of Hormuz or the South China Sea.

49008.d: FY-3E made history as the world's first operational polar weather satellite in a dawn-dusk orbit — crossing poles during sunrise and sunset when atmospheric changes are most rapid. ECMWF in Europe calls its unique sounding data 'transformational' for their forecast models. China pioneered an orbital slot no other nation had dared attempt.

49011.d: The fifth first-generation Tianlian satellite, extending China's orbital communications relay network. Tianlian-1-05 provides essential connectivity for the Tiangong Space Station crew — relaying voice communications, experimental data, medical monitoring, and high-definition video between astronauts and China's mission control in Beijing. The satellite also supports China's deep space missions and robotic scientific payloads in low Earth orbit.

49055.d: The newest Embratel Star One satellite, providing next-generation Ka-band high-throughput broadband across Brazil and Latin America. Star One D2 targets Brazil's exploding demand for satellite internet driven by the country's digital inclusion programs aiming to connect schools, health clinics, and government buildings in remote Amazon and interior regions where terrestrial broadband is decades away.

49056.d: The world's first fully software-defined commercial satellite — one that can be completely reprogrammed while in orbit. Every aspect of Eutelsat Quantum's operation can be reconfigured from the ground: its eight beams can be reshaped and redirected to any coverage area, its frequency allocations can be changed, its power distribution adjusted, and it can even detect and automatically counter jamming attempts by switching frequencies in real time. US defense contractor Peraton holds the majority of its capacity, using it for military communications and surveillance missions that change too frequently for a fixed-payload satellite to accommodate. Built by Airbus with ESA support, Quantum represents the future of satellite design — a universal platform that can be any satellite its operators need it to be.

49062.d: A recent Chinese military communications satellite providing enhanced capacity to the PLA's secure communications network. China's military satellite communications architecture has evolved substantially — from basic voice relay to high-throughput digital networks capable of supporting unmanned systems, intelligence data relay, and the full bandwidth demands of modern military operations.

49070.d: The twin to Pleiades Neo 3, flying offset by 180 degrees for twice-daily revisit. Together the pair provides 30 cm imagery of any target on Earth within 24 hours — a capability that until recently required classified military satellite access. Commercial space has permanently changed who can see what.

49115.d: The seventh TJS satellite, continuing China's classified geostationary military program. China has launched more classified GEO satellites than any other nation in recent years, reflecting the PLA's view of space as an operational warfighting domain rather than solely a support environment. TJS-7's mission remains officially undisclosed, but its rapid succession in a growing series suggests operational military communications are the primary purpose.

49125.d: A Chinese direct-to-home broadcasting satellite providing Ku-band TV distribution across China. ChinaSat-9B delivers Chinese state and commercial television channels to homes in China's rural and remote regions, including Tibet, Xinjiang, and Inner Mongolia — areas where satellite TV is often the only access to national television programming and a key tool for national cultural cohesion in linguistically and ethnically diverse territories.

49260.d: The latest Landsat maintains Earth's most valuable land surface archive — 50 continuous years of consistent imagery of every continent. Scientists have used the combined record to track loss of 40% of Earth's temperate forests, growth of 37 of the 40 largest cities, and retreat of virtually every glacier on the planet. Arguably the most scientifically productive Earth observation program in history.

49330.d: The most operationally significant Chinese GEO technology demonstration satellite. In 2022, Shijian-21 successfully docked with a dead Chinese satellite in graveyard orbit and relocated it to a different parking slot — demonstrating China's ability to maneuver non-cooperative objects in space. This capability — grasping, moving, and releasing another satellite — is directly applicable to removing adversary satellites from their operational orbits. It was observed by US Space Force and widely discussed as a milestone in Chinese anti-satellite capability development.
49330.t: Technology Demo / Satellite Servicing / Anti-Satellite Capable

49333.d: The newest generation of French military communications satellites, providing dramatic improvements in capacity, security, and anti-jam performance. Syracuse-4A uses quantum key distribution-compatible encryption and advanced waveforms designed to defeat adversary electronic warfare. France's investment in next-generation military space capabilities reflects the growing importance of the space domain in European security policy.
49333.a: French Ministry of Defence / Thales Alenia

49336.d: Japan's first quasi-zenith satellite replacement — part of the Michibiki (Guidance) constellation that augments GPS with Japan-specific signals. Quasi-zenith satellites follow a figure-8 orbit that keeps them nearly overhead Japan for most of the day, solving a problem unique to Japan's urban geography: tall buildings in Tokyo and Osaka block GPS signals at low elevation angles. Michibiki's high-elevation signals cut through urban canyons where GPS fails, enabling centimeter-level navigation for autonomous vehicles across Japanese cities.

49505.d: A Chinese military or dual-use communications satellite operating in geostationary orbit. The '1D' designation in the Zhongxing series indicates a classified or government-priority payload. China's satellite communications architecture deliberately blends civil and military functions, making it difficult for outside analysts to definitively characterize individual satellites as purely civil or purely military.

49817.d: A US Space Force technology demonstration satellite hosted on a geostationary platform as part of the Space Technology Program. STPSAT-6 tests advanced communications technologies, space environment sensors, and experimental payloads in the harsh GEO radiation environment. The STPSAT program provides a flight-test pathway for emerging technologies before they're incorporated into operational military satellites.

49954.d: X-ray polarimetry — measuring how X-rays from cosmic sources are oriented — was theorized as a powerful tool for 60 years but never successfully done from orbit until IXPE. Launched in 2021, NASA's Imaging X-ray Polarimetry Explorer measures not just where X-rays come from but how they're polarized, revealing the geometry of magnetic fields around black holes, neutron stars, and supernova remnants. Its first results showed that the Crab Nebula's pulsar jet is far more ordered and structured than any model had predicted.

50001.d: Russia's latest Express satellite, continuing the modernization of the country's commercial satellite fleet under difficult conditions created by Western technology restrictions. Express AMU-7 covers Russia's most densely populated regions with Ku-band TV distribution and Ka-band broadband, supporting the Russian government's goal of extending satellite internet access to rural populations across the country.

50002.d: One of Russia's newest commercial communications satellites, part of the modernization of Russia's aging geostationary commercial fleet. Express AMU-3 provides Ku and Ka-band services for Russian customers, replacing capacity from earlier satellites approaching end of life. Russia has faced significant challenges procuring satellite components under sanctions, making domestic satellite manufacturing capability increasingly strategic.

50005.d: The second Tianlian-2 satellite, completing the initial next-generation pair. Tianlian 2-02 significantly expands coverage to ensure Tiangong crew have nearly continuous data relay contact regardless of the station's orbital position. China's growing human spaceflight program — planning longer missions and more frequent crew rotations — demands the communication infrastructure that Tianlian-2 provides.

50212.d: Turkey's Ka-band high-throughput satellite, the most powerful Turkish communications satellite ever built. Turksat-5B provides broadband internet services across Turkey with a capacity exceeding all previous Turkish satellites combined, targeting Turkey's ambitious national broadband connectivity goals and competing with terrestrial fiber in underserved areas. Turkey's Turksat is positioning itself as a provider of satellite services to Central Asian and Middle Eastern nations along historical Silk Road connectivity corridors.

50319.d: The first of Inmarsat's sixth-generation satellites — a massive 9-ton spacecraft combining L-band safety services with Ka-band broadband in a single platform. Inmarsat-6 represents the future of maritime communications: a single satellite providing both the regulatory-mandated GMDSS emergency communication and gigabit-class commercial broadband, making it the most capable maritime communications satellite ever built. Its L-band payload maintains continuity of international maritime safety services while its Ka-band payload transforms what ships can do with connectivity.

50574.d: Another TJS satellite observed performing anomalous maneuvers and releasing objects in geostationary orbit. TJS-9 attracted significant attention from US Space Force and independent analysts who tracked its proximity operations — approaching other satellites and releasing sub-satellite objects. These activities are consistent with China testing counter-satellite inspection, electronic intelligence, or jamming capabilities in the geostationary belt.

51280.d: A classified US government satellite in geostationary orbit launched in 2021. Its mission is unacknowledged. Independent space tracking organizations monitor its orbit, which is consistent with national security communications or intelligence collection payloads routinely placed in GEO by the NRO and US Space Force. The classified nature reflects the sensitivity of capabilities that peer adversaries would find most valuable to understand and counter.

51444.d: The second COSMO-SkyMed Second Generation satellite completes Italy's upgraded radar constellation, achieving sub-6-hour revisit over any Mediterranean target. Italy now operates one of the world's most capable Earth observation systems — serving both military intelligence and civilian environmental monitoring from the same two-satellite constellation.

51850.d: The newest GOES-West satellite provides the most detailed surveillance of the Pacific Ocean and western North America ever achieved from geostationary orbit. It simultaneously watches for California wildfires, tracks Pacific typhoons toward Hawaii, monitors Alaskan volcanoes, and detects Rocky Mountain lightning — all in real time from 35,800 km up.

52255.d: A Chinese C-band communications satellite extending ChinaSat's commercial coverage. Zhongxing-6D serves a combination of Chinese broadcaster requirements, corporate network customers, and government services across the Asia-Pacific. China's commercial satellite operators have benefited from government support that allows them to offer competitive pricing in international markets.

52754.d: The third satellite of Cluster 5. Each successive HawkEye 360 cluster incorporates software-defined radio improvements that expand frequency coverage and improve sensitivity — detecting weaker signals and covering new frequency bands used by emerging communication systems.

52756.d: Second satellite of Cluster 5. HawkEye 360's growing constellation attracted significant US intelligence community interest — the ability to geolocate adversary radar systems, communications links, and GPS jammers from commercial satellites that are politically less sensitive than government intelligence assets.

52757.d: First satellite of HawkEye 360 Cluster 5. By Cluster 5, the revisit time was short enough to track vessels moving at sea — following a ship's position through multiple cluster overflights to build its track over time, even without any AIS signal.

52817.d: Egypt's newest broadcasting satellite, providing additional Ku-band capacity at 7°W and extending coverage into Sub-Saharan Africa. NileSat-301 supports the continuing explosion of Arabic satellite television channels and the transition to HD and 4K broadcasting across Egypt and the Arab world. Egypt's NileSat has become a commercial satellite operator of regional significance, providing services to governments and broadcasters across the Arab world and Africa.

52903.d: An Indian commercial communications satellite entirely leased to Tata Play (formerly Tata Sky) for direct-to-home television distribution across India. CMS-02 represents a new commercial model for ISRO — leasing complete satellite capacity to a private operator rather than managing commercial services directly. Tata Play uses it to deliver its premium HD and 4K television channels to millions of Indian subscribers.

52904.d: Malaysia's newest and highest-throughput satellite, providing Ka-band broadband alongside traditional broadcasting services. MEASAT 3D reflects Malaysia's ambition to address Southeast Asia's massive unmet broadband demand — connecting the rural populations of Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines who lack terrestrial internet access. Malaysia's satellite sector aims to become a regional broadband hub as Southeast Asian digital economies continue to accelerate.

52912.d: China's third Gaofen-12 SAR satellite densifies the radar surveillance constellation. The rapid deployment pace — multiple per year — demonstrates China's industrial capacity to mass-produce capable radar reconnaissance satellites, changing the global balance of space-based surveillance capability.

52940.d: The Wide Field of View satellite — one of the few classified US military GEO satellites with a partially acknowledged purpose. WFOV is assessed as a space domain awareness payload, using wide-angle optical sensors to monitor other satellites in the GEO belt. As adversary anti-satellite capabilities have grown, the US Space Force has invested heavily in surveillance of the geostationary arc itself, watching for threatening maneuvers by foreign spacecraft.

53100.d: The third second-generation Tianlian satellite, working toward full global data relay coverage. Tianlian 2-03 covers the Atlantic and South American sectors, filling geographic gaps in the first-generation constellation. China's space station program is now permanently crewed, making continuous communications not optional but operationally essential — a crew medical emergency or critical system fault requires immediate contact with ground teams.

53105.d: The sequel to LARES, made from niobium alloy and orbiting in a specially chosen inclination to cancel out gravitational perturbations that complicate frame-dragging measurements. When combined with LARES data, it will test Einstein's frame-dragging prediction to within 0.2% — a remarkable precision for a gravitational experiment, and the inaugural payload of ESA's Vega-C rocket.

53355.d: The sixth and final planned SBIRS satellite, completing the global missile warning constellation. SBIRS GEO-6 brings redundancy and enhanced coverage to the system as the US faces a proliferating ballistic missile threat from multiple peer adversaries. Together the SBIRS constellation provides 24/7 coverage of every ballistic missile launch on Earth, making it impossible for any nation to conduct a surprise nuclear strike without immediate American awareness.

53813.d: A Chinese high-throughput satellite extending China's government communications capabilities. Zhongxing-1E provides secure and priority communications capacity for Chinese government agencies, state enterprises, and infrastructure monitoring applications across China's territory and overseas Chinese interests — the digital nervous system of China's expanding global economic and political footprint.

54033.d: Angola's second attempt at a national communications satellite — the first, AngoSat-1, suffered a power system failure and was lost in 2018. AngoSat-2, built with Russian cooperation, provides C and Ku-band communications services across Angola and the broader African continent. Angola's persistence in pursuing satellite capability despite the AngoSat-1 failure reflects the continent's acute need for satellite connectivity — terrestrial infrastructure serving vast sub-Saharan African territories remains chronically insufficient.

54219.d: A hosted payload on a commercial GEO satellite testing advanced technologies for the US Space Force. The Long Duration Propulsion Experiment program validates next-generation propulsion and satellite bus technologies in the operational GEO environment — reducing technical risk for future military GEO satellites by demonstrating new capabilities on commercial platforms rather than purpose-built government spacecraft.

54230.d: A Chinese communications satellite providing C and Ku-band services across China and the Asia-Pacific. Zhongxing-19 serves the expanding Chinese commercial satellite communications market, which faces competition from global operators but benefits from government preference policies that ensure state-owned enterprises like ChinaSat maintain their position in China's domestic satellite services market.

54234.d: When NOAA-21 joined NOAA-20 in orbit, their combined data improved 48-hour temperature forecasts over the entire Northern Hemisphere — the equivalent of adding an entirely new global observing network. Its data is in every major weather model running right now. When you see a 5-day forecast, NOAA-21 helped build it.

54743.d: Europe's generational leap in geostationary meteorology. MTG-I1's Lightning Imager is the first ever on a European weather satellite, detecting every lightning flash across Europe, Africa, and the Atlantic simultaneously. Meteorologists say it extends severe weather warning lead times by 30 minutes — 30 minutes that can mean the difference between shelter and exposure in a violent storm.

54754.d: The Surface Water and Ocean Topography satellite maps 120 km wide swaths of every lake, river, and ocean simultaneously — the first time scientists can observe how water moves across the entire surface of Earth. For the first time, flood forecasting models can see where the water is going, not just where it has been. A fundamental change in how we manage water for billions of people.

55131.d: China's Shijian-23 technology demonstration satellite, continuing the series of large GEO experiments that test advanced propulsion, communications, and satellite bus technologies. The SJ series has become China's proving ground for next-generation GEO capabilities — the satellites that demonstrate technologies eventually incorporated into China's operational military and commercial space platforms.

55239.d: APStar's newest commercial satellite, extending the operator's Ka-band coverage for maritime and aviation connectivity. APStar-6E reflects the growing demand for high-speed satellite internet from Chinese commercial shipping and aviation industries. China's Belt and Maritime Silk Road shipping interests across the Indian Ocean, Pacific, and beyond create specific demand for reliable connectivity along routes where coverage from Western operators may be limited.

55263.d: A classified US government satellite in geostationary orbit launched in 2023. Orbital tracking reveals its GEO position but not its mission, which remains officially unacknowledged. The growing pace of classified US GEO satellite launches reflects the increasing importance of the geostationary orbital regime as a domain of strategic competition between spacefaring nations.

55264.d: Another Long Duration Propulsion Experiment satellite, continuing the US Space Force's program of testing advanced technologies hosted on commercial GEO platforms. This approach dramatically reduces the cost and schedule of technology validation — a commercial satellite carries experimental military payloads as secondary passengers, providing real operational environment testing at a fraction of dedicated satellite costs.

55324.d: The second satellite of Cluster 6. The Ukraine conflict demonstrated the military value of space-based RF geolocation — locating radars, jammers, and communication networks from orbit without risking aircraft or personnel — accelerating government demand for commercial RF intelligence products.

55326.d: The third satellite of Cluster 6, representing the first commercial system capable of near-global, near-continuous radio frequency monitoring from orbit — a capability that has opened new markets in maritime security, spectrum management, and signals intelligence.

55327.d: First satellite of HawkEye 360 Cluster 6. As the constellation approaches full deployment, global revisit times are measured in hours rather than days — transforming from an occasional snapshot of radio emissions to a near-continuous monitoring system for global radio frequency activity.

55508.d: Hispasat's next-generation Americas satellite, featuring a fully flexible high-throughput payload that can allocate capacity dynamically across coverage zones.
