Amazonas Nexus supports the continued expansion of Brazilian satellite broadband — a market that has grown dramatically as the pandemic forced remote work and education into rural areas previously served only by voice satellite. Its flexible payload adjusts coverage and power allocation in real time to match demand patterns that shift seasonally and daily.

55686.d: A Chinese high-throughput communications satellite providing Ka-band broadband across China. Zhongxing-26 addresses the rapidly growing demand for satellite broadband in China's underserved western and rural regions. China's government has invested heavily in satellite internet connectivity as part of its digital infrastructure programs, and Zhongxing-26 is part of that national connectivity strategy.

55841.d: Russia's most controversial satellite — and the most closely watched object in the geostationary belt. Luch-5X, also known as Olymp-K 2, has made a series of unexplained close approaches to commercial and government satellites operated by other nations, maneuvering to within a few kilometers of targets including Intelsat satellites, European military spacecraft, and US government satellites. Russia has not explained the purpose of these maneuvers. Independent analysts assess Olymp-K 2 as a signals intelligence or electronic warfare satellite testing proximity operations capabilities against foreign spacecraft — the most concerning behavior exhibited by any satellite in GEO.

55912.d: The second Gaofen-13 geostationary optical imaging satellite, doubling China's persistent surveillance capability from GEO. Together with Gaofen-4 and Gaofen-13 01, China has built a small but growing constellation of geostationary optical satellites that can maintain continuous visual observation of strategic regions — a capability no other nation possesses at operational scale. China's ability to persistently monitor the Taiwan Strait, South China Sea, and Indian border regions from GEO provides a persistent intelligence advantage.

55973.d: One of China's Tianmu commercial weather satellites using GPS radio occultation to probe the atmosphere. Each GPS signal bent through the atmosphere becomes a precise temperature and humidity profile — a cosmic thermometer with no cloud cover limitation. The constellation's goal: atmospheric profiles every 90 minutes globally, ten times more frequent than any existing system.

55974.d: Tianmu #4, building China's commercial GPS occultation weather network. The constellation already outnumbers all government-operated radio occultation satellites combined, reshaping who controls global weather data. A commercial entity quietly displacing government monopolies on atmospheric intelligence.

55975.d: Tianmu #5 continues China's commercial weather data revolution. Radio occultation profiles each GPS bending through the atmosphere from surface to 60 km, through any clouds, in complete darkness. The most all-weather atmospheric measurement technique ever invented — and China is deploying it at commercial scale.

55976.d: Tianmu #6 demonstrates China's pivot from state-operated space infrastructure toward a commercial data economy. Tianmu data is sold to national meteorological services globally — including countries without their own weather satellites — giving China influence over forecasting infrastructure in dozens of nations.

56191.d: First satellite of HawkEye 360 Cluster 7. The seventh cluster incorporates upgraded receivers capable of characterizing signals in greater detail — moving from simply locating emitters to identifying them by their technical characteristics, a jump from 'there's a signal here' to 'this is a specific radar type.'

56193.d: Second satellite of Cluster 7. By this stage HawkEye 360 had signed partnerships with multiple national coast guards and maritime authorities — providing commercial satellite RF geolocation as an operational service rather than an occasional data product. Commercial space intelligence is becoming routine infrastructure.

56200.d: The third satellite of Cluster 7, completing the latest addition to HawkEye 360's constellation. From three Pathfinder satellites in 2018 to 21+ operational spacecraft by 2023 — HawkEye 360 demonstrated the commercial viability of space-based RF geolocation and established itself as the global leader in a brand-new commercial intelligence category.

56232.d: China's first precipitation-focused polar satellite carries a dual-frequency precipitation radar — the first such instrument on any Chinese spacecraft. Combined with a microwave imager, it measures rainfall rates and raindrop size distribution globally, transforming China's typhoon rainfall tracking and its contribution to international precipitation monitoring.

56370.d: The first of Viasat's third-generation constellation — designed to deliver 1 Tbps of capacity over the Americas, 10 times ViaSat-2's throughput. After launch in 2023, a reflector deployment anomaly prevented ViaSat-3 Americas from achieving its intended capacity, providing only a fraction of planned throughput. The partial failure was one of the costliest satellite anomalies in commercial history — a humbling reminder that even proven operators can face crippling technical failures at the frontier of satellite technology.

56371.d: Northrop Grumman's second mission extension vehicle in geostationary orbit, operating as the latest commercial satellite servicing spacecraft. Following the success of MEV-1 and MEV-2, Arcturus represents the next generation of satellite life extension — potentially capable of serving multiple clients sequentially. The emergence of a commercial satellite servicing industry is transforming GEO economics: operators can now plan for satellite life extension rather than replacement, significantly reducing the cost per year of GEO communications service.

56372.d: A commercial geostationary satellite providing communications services. GS-1 is part of the continuing diversification of GEO operators beyond the traditional large commercial satellite companies — smaller, specialized operators increasingly finding niches in the GEO arc serving specific geographic markets, frequency bands, or customer types that larger constellations don't address efficiently.

56564.d: A newer BeiDou-3 geostationary satellite providing additional capacity and redundancy for China's global navigation system. As BeiDou has gained international adoption — now used across much of Asia, Africa, and along the Belt and Road — the GEO component's Short Message Service has found new applications in maritime safety, disaster response, and government communications across dozens of nations.

56757.d: Arabsat's newest satellite, positioned at 26°E — the 'Hot Bird of the Arab world' — where more than 500 channels are broadcast to over 100 million viewers. Arabsat-7B provides the next-generation capacity to support 4K and future 8K broadcasting as Arab audiences upgrade to higher-definition programming. The 26°E orbital slot has become the most-watched satellite position in the Arab world, rivaling Europe's Astra 19.2°E as a content concentration point.

56759.d: The first second-generation NavIC satellite, incorporating significantly upgraded atomic clocks and a new L1 signal compatible with GPS and Galileo. NVS-01 enables dual-frequency reception — comparing L1 and L5 signals to cancel ionospheric errors that limit single-frequency GPS accuracy to 3-5 meters. With dual-frequency NavIC, India's navigation system achieves sub-meter accuracy autonomously, without augmentation from ground networks.

57045.d: Indonesia's largest and most powerful communications satellite — a national broadband project providing Ka-band internet to over 150,000 public service points across Indonesia's remote islands. SATRIA (Satellite Indonesia Raya) specifically targets schools, health clinics, government offices, and military outposts in areas unreachable by fiber or 4G — the digitally excluded population of the world's fourth most populous nation. Indonesia's government invested in SATRIA as infrastructure equivalent to building roads through its island geography.

57166.d: Russia's third Meteor-M No.2 expands Arctic polar weather coverage. Each new Meteor-M improves Russia's capacity to track sea ice conditions along the Northern Sea Route — which Russia is developing as a commercially viable alternative to the Suez Canal for European-Asian shipping as Arctic ice retreats.

57213.d: Germany's experimental communications satellite named after the physicist who first proved the existence of electromagnetic waves. Heinrich Hertz carries experimental payloads testing Q/V band communications (frequencies above 40 GHz) and optical laser links — technologies needed for future next-generation satellite communications. Germany's DLR and several commercial partners use it to validate technologies that will appear in operational satellites a decade from now, filling the gap between laboratory and full-scale mission that technology demonstration satellites uniquely address.

57214.d: The companion to Syracuse-4A, completing France's next-generation military communications pair. Together they provide France with the most capable European-independent military satellite communications system, supporting the full range of French defense activities from nuclear deterrence to special operations. France and Germany are the only European nations operating fully independent strategic military satellite constellations.
57214.a: French Ministry of Defence / Thales Alenia

57391.d: Spire Global's LEMUR-2 DEVERILL-M-T is a 3U CubeSat in the LEMUR-2 constellation simultaneously tracking weather, ships, and aircraft from a single compact spacecraft. The '-M-T' suffix indicates a modified variant with enhanced radio frequency capabilities beyond the baseline LEMUR-2 design. Spire names its satellites with anarchic creativity that scales with volume — when you operate hundreds of satellites, you need naming that doesn't run out. DEVERILL-M-T's GPS radio occultation soundings profile the atmosphere from surface to stratosphere, feeding weather forecast models that improve accuracy over oceans where no ground instruments exist and where most of Earth's major storm systems are born.

57399.d: Tianmu #7 enters a new orbital plane, increasing the constellation's global revisit frequency. The target: atmospheric profiles every 90 minutes — making weather data as current as a live traffic feed. When achieved, it would be the most frequent global atmospheric measurement in human history.

57400.d: Tianmu #8, quietly becoming one of the most significant new inputs to global weather prediction this decade — rivaling multi-billion-dollar traditional weather satellites at a fraction of the cost. The economics of atmospheric sounding changed permanently.

57401.d: Tianmu #9 reflects China's extraordinary commercial space acceleration. Five years ago, commercial weather satellites were purely American and European. The Tianmu constellation's rapid deployment shows how quickly China's private space companies built capability once they gained government and venture capital backing.

57402.d: Tianmu #10. Radio occultation has become the single most valuable new data source for weather forecasting this decade — improving short-range forecasts more than any other advance since geostationary weather satellites appeared in the 1970s.

57479.d: The most powerful commercial communications satellite ever built when launched in 2023 — a 9-metric-ton behemoth with 500 Gbps of total capacity, more than double the throughput of the next most capable commercial satellite at the time. Jupiter 3 provides internet access to rural North and South America through Hughes Network Systems' HughesNet service, using hundreds of Ka-band spot beams to concentrate power where customers are. Its 18 kW solar arrays generate more power than any previous commercial satellite. Jupiter 3 shifts broadband from a subscription service to a commodity for millions of unserved rural Americans, Canadians, and Latin Americans.

57490.d: FY-3F complements China's polar weather fleet in the afternoon orbital slot, building toward a full Fengyun-3 constellation independent of US or European data. Upgraded ozone monitoring and stratospheric sounding extend China's atmospheric science capabilities across the full vertical atmosphere.

57624.d: A Chinese land detection satellite in geostationary orbit, officially designed for land and geological monitoring. From GEO, a land detection satellite can continuously observe agricultural conditions, desertification trends, and natural disaster development across a fixed geographic region — capabilities useful for both environmental monitoring and persistent agricultural intelligence that drives food security policy.

57654.d: The fourth Gaofen-12 SAR satellite. China's proliferating radar constellation has eliminated single-satellite vulnerabilities and established persistent all-weather coverage of strategic global targets. Space-based radar intelligence, once exclusive to the United States, is now a Chinese operational reality.

58204.d: The tenth TJS satellite, representing the continued and accelerating pace of China's classified GEO military program. With more than 20 TJS satellites now in orbit, the series has grown from experimental designations to what analysts consider one of the world's largest classified military geostationary constellations. China's continued investment in TJS capacity reflects the PLA Strategic Support Force's prioritization of space-based military capabilities.

58253.d: One of China's newest commercial C-band satellites, extending its coverage into new markets. Zhongxing-6E reflects China's continued investment in C-band commercial capacity at a time when many Western operators are transitioning toward Ka-band high-throughput systems — recognizing that C-band remains essential for reliability in tropical markets and for professional video distribution applications that favor its characteristics.

58582.d: An unusual entry in China's Yaogan Earth observation series — operating in geostationary orbit rather than the low-altitude orbits typical of the Yaogan surveillance constellation. A geostationary Yaogan satellite provides persistent coverage of a fixed region rather than the global sweeping coverage of LEO systems, suggesting a specialized intelligence collection role targeting a specific geographic area with continuous, unbroken observation capability.

58584.d: The second Arktika-M completes Russia's highly elliptical Arctic monitoring pair — the first system in history providing continuous geostationary-quality coverage of the North Pole 24 hours a day. As the melting Arctic generates increasingly unpredictable weather affecting the entire Northern Hemisphere, this capability is becoming indispensable.

58645.d: Tianmu #11, one of four launched together accelerating build-out of China's commercial atmospheric sounding network. The ability to mass-produce and batch-launch radio occultation satellites is changing the economics of weather data globally — and China is leading the change.

58646.d: Tianmu #12 deployed alongside three sisters in a rideshare launch. Each compact satellite provides atmospheric soundings that most improve forecasts over the world's least-observed ocean regions, where model errors accumulate fastest and storm track forecasts diverge the most.

58647.d: Tianmu #13 adds tropical coverage where it matters most. GPS occultation through moist tropical air reveals moisture structures that determine whether a hurricane intensifies into a monster or weakens into a rainmaker — the exact data that forecasters have lacked for decades.

58648.d: Tianmu #14. China's rapid commercial weather satellite deployment mirrors its infrastructure strategy in other sectors — build fast, build redundantly, achieve independence from foreign data providers. Weather data sovereignty is becoming as strategically important as energy independence.

58660.d: Tianmu #19 fills gaps in tropical plane coverage. GPS occultation through warm, moisture-laden tropical air reveals the humidity structures that determine whether a tropical disturbance becomes a catastrophic hurricane or fizzles into a rainstorm — the key data forecasters have lacked for decades.

58661.d: Tianmu #20. A geopolitical question is forming: will weather agencies in Europe, Japan, and the US build competing commercial ecosystems, or become data customers of Chinese networks? The answer will shape global meteorology infrastructure for a generation.

58662.d: Tianmu #21. Each satellite measures hundreds of atmospheric profiles per day — complete vertical slices of temperature, pressure, and humidity from the ground to the stratosphere. Across the full Tianmu constellation, it represents one of the most prolific atmospheric measurement programs in history.

58663.d: The twenty-second Tianmu satellite. For the first time, a non-Western commercial entity is positioning itself as a primary weather data supplier to meteorological services globally — including in Africa and Southeast Asia where coverage has historically been critically poor.

58698.d: Sweden's high-throughput military and government communications satellite, developed by Ovzon to provide mobile connectivity for military users and government agencies. Ovzon-3 targets a specific niche: encrypted, high-speed broadband for mobile terminals used by military units in the field, intelligence agencies, and emergency services that need reliable connectivity without large ground infrastructure. Built to survive the interference-rich electromagnetic environment of military operations.

58700.d: Tianmu #15 in a new orbital plane, dramatically increasing global revisit frequency — moving from a useful supplement toward a primary atmospheric sounding system for numerical weather prediction models worldwide.

58701.d: Tianmu #16, particularly valuable for weather models over the Southern Ocean — where conventional observations are almost nonexistent and forecast errors accumulate faster than anywhere else on Earth.

58702.d: Tianmu #17, approaching the temporal resolution that makes weather data actionable in near-real-time — the equivalent of going from one daily weather balloon launch to continuous atmospheric monitoring at thousands of locations simultaneously.

58703.d: Tianmu #18. The constellation now produces more GPS occultation soundings per day than any single national agency's system — a remarkable achievement for a commercial entity that barely existed five years before.

58990.d: India's most advanced meteorological satellite combines 19-channel atmospheric sounding and 6-channel imaging in one spacecraft. It positions India to track monsoon dynamics and Bay of Bengal cyclones with accuracy comparable to Japan's Himawari — critical where monsoon forecasting directly affects food production for 1.4 billion people.

59020.d: One of the more recent TJS satellites, part of an accelerating Chinese classified GEO program. The TJS series has continued launching at a pace of several per year despite international attention on the series's activities. China's persistent investment in classified GEO satellites demonstrates that the geostationary belt has become a domain of active military competition, mirroring US investments in AEHF, SBIRS, and space domain awareness satellites.

59051.d: Russia's fourth Meteor-M No.2 expands polar coverage as Soviet-era spacecraft retire. Russia's 11-time-zone territory requires domestic weather satellite capability for reasons as much political as scientific — no other nation's forecasts will reliably cover Russian interests as well as Russian satellites.

59065.d: Iran's domestically built Earth observation satellite, developed despite international sanctions restricting access to many space technologies. Pars-1 demonstrates Iran's determination to achieve space-based observation capability independent of foreign supply chains — a milestone in Iran's technology sovereignty program.

59069.d: The first satellite in China's Hulianwan ('Internet of Things') commercial GEO constellation — one of the first dedicated IoT satellites designed for geostationary orbit. Unlike LEO IoT constellations (Starlink, Iridium), a GEO IoT satellite covers a fixed region continuously from a single spacecraft. Hulianwan Gaogui-01 targets China's vast agricultural and industrial monitoring market, connecting remote sensors across Chinese territory that lack terrestrial connectivity.

59371.d: Russia's latest resource monitoring satellite provides hyperspectral and high-resolution imagery across Russia's 17 million square kilometers. Russia's territory — containing the world's largest forest reserve, major mineral deposits, and the changing Arctic — makes space-based observation essential for national resource management at a scale no other monitoring method can match.

59625.d: The first satellite in Airbus's LEGION hyperspectral constellation brings science-grade hyperspectral imaging to a commercial daily-revisit service. Measuring 400+ spectral bands per pixel, LEGION identifies pollution events, maps mineral resources, and detects crop diseases before visible symptoms appear — generating orders of magnitude more value per pixel than standard color imagery.

59626.d: The second LEGION hyperspectral satellite builds the commercial constellation Airbus positions as the successor to traditional multispectral Earth observation. By detecting hundreds of spectral signatures invisible to standard cameras, LEGION can verify environmental compliance, authenticate agricultural claims, and map resource distribution across entire continents.

59915.d: Pakistan's newest communications satellite, significantly expanding the country's geostationary capacity. PakSat-MM1 provides enhanced broadband and broadcasting services for Pakistan's 220 million population — one of the world's largest underserved satellite markets. Pakistan's investment in new satellite capacity reflects the government's digital Pakistan initiative and the growing demand for satellite connectivity in rural and mountainous regions beyond the reach of terrestrial networks.

60133.d: The fourth and final GOES-R series satellite, GOES-19 completed America's next-generation geostationary fleet as GOES-East in 2024. Its Advanced Baseline Imager has redefined Western Hemisphere weather forecasting. When you see a satellite image of an Atlantic hurricane on the news, it came from this spacecraft or its siblings.

60179.d: A recent Chinese communications satellite providing Ka-band high-throughput broadband across China and neighboring countries. Zhongxing-3A is part of China's push to deploy next-generation broadband satellite capacity domestically, reducing reliance on foreign high-throughput satellite services and building China's position as a satellite internet provider across its Belt and Road partner countries.

60182.d: Japan's ALOS-4 carries the most powerful L-band SAR ever flown — 3 times the power of ALOS-2 with 1-meter capability. L-band radar penetrates forest canopies to measure the trees beneath — enabling forest carbon stock measurement from orbit that will underpin the next generation of verified carbon credit markets worth hundreds of billions of dollars.

60233.d: Turkey's first domestically designed and built communications satellite — a milestone comparable to India's ARSAT or Argentina's ARSAT programs. Turksat-6A was built by Turkish Aerospace Industries (TAI) using primarily domestic technology, fulfilling Turkey's ambition to become an independent satellite manufacturing nation. The satellite provides Ku-band capacity for Turkish communications services and represents decades of investment in building indigenous satellite engineering capability.

60327.d: The second Hulianwan GEO IoT satellite, expanding China's smart connectivity network. Hulianwan-02 provides M2M data collection across Chinese farms, mines, pipelines, and utility infrastructure — bringing real-time monitoring to remote Chinese infrastructure previously beyond the reach of affordable connectivity. China's enormous unmonitored infrastructure creates massive demand for satellite IoT services.

60452.d: LEGION #3 builds daily hyperspectral coverage. Each satellite builds time-series data revealing change — crop growth curves predicting yield months before harvest, industrial emission patterns identifying violations, and ecosystem health trends driven by invisible atmospheric changes.

60453.d: The fourth LEGION satellite approaches daily global hyperspectral coverage. As a commercial product, it is creating entirely new markets for space-based chemical and biological intelligence about Earth's surface — seeing the world's chemistry from orbit for the first time at global scale.

60471.d: One of Spire Global's LEMUR-2 constellation — compact 3U CubeSats that do three remarkable things simultaneously from a single spacecraft the size of a shoebox. Each LEMUR-2 collects GPS radio occultation data to probe atmospheric temperature and humidity globally, listens to AIS transponders from ships at sea to track maritime traffic, and monitors ADS-B signals from aircraft. The name TOMATOKECHUP continues Spire's tradition of giving constellation satellites names that are memorable precisely because they're absurd. With hundreds of LEMUR satellites in orbit, Spire has built one of the world's most prolific atmospheric sounding networks — and this is one node in that planetary instrument.

60606.d: One of China's newest commercial communications satellites, continuing the expansion of the Zhongxing fleet. ChinaSat's commercial satellite expansion reflects China's ambition to become a major provider of satellite services across Asia, Africa, and Latin America — markets where Western operators have traditionally dominated but where Chinese pricing and political relationships are creating new competitive dynamics.

60989.d: The third Sentinel-2 ensures continuity of 10-meter land monitoring well into the 2030s. With three satellites, the combined constellation provides global land coverage every 2-3 days — fast enough to track vegetation across a full growing season, detect new deforestation within days, and monitor every major agricultural region continuously.

61503.d: The third Hulianwan GEO IoT satellite, continuing China's development of geostationary machine-to-machine connectivity. From GEO, these satellites provide continuous coverage of a fixed region without the latency and coverage gaps of LEO IoT systems — making them well-suited for applications like agricultural monitoring, wildlife tracking, and remote infrastructure management across China's vast western territories.

61733.d: Japan's third Defense Satellite Network satellite, providing secure X-band communications for the Japan Self-Defense Forces. Kirameki-3 ('Twinkle' in Japanese) supports Japanese maritime, ground, and air forces with protected communications for coalition operations with the US and for independent national defense missions. Japan's investment in dedicated military communications reflects the country's evolving security posture and reinterpretation of its pacifist constitution.

61735.d: Russia's ionospheric monitoring satellite measures electron density in the layer that reflects radio signals and enables over-the-horizon communications. Solar storms distort it, disrupting GPS, HF radio, and satellite links. Ionosfera-M provides measurements needed to forecast and mitigate disruptions to critical infrastructure from power grids to aviation.

61736.d: The companion to Ionosfera-M 1, together providing the first Russian two-satellite ionospheric monitoring system distinguishing spatial from temporal variations. Flying in complementary orbits, the pair builds the three-dimensional ionospheric picture critical for maintaining GLONASS accuracy during solar disturbances at solar maximum.

61910.d: A next-generation Korean commercial communications satellite extending KT SAT's regional coverage. Koreasat-6A provides Ku and Ka-band services for Korean broadcasting, enterprise networks, maritime communications, and aviation broadband. South Korea's high-technology maritime industry — one of the world's largest shipbuilding sectors — creates specific demand for maritime satellite communications that Koreasat-6A helps meet.

62006.d: Australia's newest commercial satellite, providing expanded Ka-band capacity for Optus's growing satellite services portfolio. Australia's satellite market continues to grow as NBN satellite internet drives infrastructure investment and commercial demand. Optus-X extends the operator's services and provides redundancy for Australia's critical satellite communications infrastructure in a country where satellite remains essential connectivity infrastructure for millions of rural residents.

62028.d: India's newest and highest-throughput commercial satellite, launched on SpaceX's Falcon 9 after ISRO's GSLV Mk III launch schedule conflicted with the satellite's delivery timeline. GSAT-N2 provides Ka-band broadband with 48 high-power spot beams covering the entire Indian subcontinent — enough throughput to support India's government Digital India broadband expansion programs and commercial satellite internet subscribers simultaneously. Its launch on an American rocket raised eyebrows but reflected ISRO's pragmatic approach to commercial timelines.

62259.d: A newer SiriusXM satellite continuing the modernization of its broadcasting constellation. SiriusXM's satellite radio service reaches subscribers from Alaska to the Caribbean, maintaining uninterrupted programming during long road trips across North America — a connectivity experience that terrestrial radio cannot replicate. SXM-9 expands the system's capacity and provides additional orbital positions for improved coverage.

62374.d: A recent TJS satellite continuing China's classified GEO military program. The series has grown beyond what any 'communication technology experiment' designation can justify — the number, pace, and observed behavior of TJS satellites indicate an operational military communications and/or intelligence collection program of considerable scale, rivaling the combined GEO military presence of the US and its allies.

62455.d: The first of NuView's revolutionary commercial synthetic aperture radar satellites in geostationary orbit — an extraordinary technical ambition. Traditional SAR satellites operate from low Earth orbit to concentrate their radar energy; NuView is attempting to operate SAR from 35,800 km altitude, which requires enormously more powerful signals. If successful, NuView Alpha would provide continuous persistent SAR surveillance of a fixed target area rather than the periodic passes of LEO SAR — fundamentally changing what persistent intelligence collection looks like.

62457.d: The companion to NuView Alpha in the world's first commercial GEO SAR constellation attempt. NuView Bravo adds a second coverage node to what could become persistent all-weather surveillance from geostationary orbit — a capability that would make it impossible to conceal large-scale military movements, industrial activity, or environmental changes from constant radar observation. Whether the technology achieves its promise will determine whether GEO SAR becomes a major commercial market.

62483.d: Thuraya's newest satellite, incorporating IoT machine-to-machine services alongside traditional voice and broadband capabilities. Thuraya-4 reflects the evolution of mobile satellite services toward connected devices — sensors, tracking systems, and industrial monitors that need occasional small data bursts rather than continuous voice calls. The satellite serves Middle Eastern and African markets where demand for satellite IoT connectivity is growing as agriculture, logistics, and resource extraction industries modernize.

62485.d: One of China's latest Shijian technology demonstration satellites in geostationary orbit. China's rapid succession of SJ technology demonstrators reflects the pace at which Chinese space technology is advancing — each SJ satellite incorporating lessons and pushing capabilities further than its predecessor. The technologies validated in the SJ program become the foundation for China's next generation of operational military and civil satellites.

62725.d: A high-resolution optical reconnaissance satellite in Saudi Arabia's growing Earth observation constellation. The Kingdom's rapid expansion of domestic space capability reduces dependence on commercial imagery providers for monitoring its vast territory, oil infrastructure, and the regional security environment.

62804.d: Part of China's rapidly expanding TJS classified GEO program. The frequency with which China is launching these satellites — sometimes multiple per year — indicates urgency in building out a capability the PLA considers operationally essential. The TJS constellation provides China with a robust, redundant, and presumably jam-resistant military communications network across the geostationary arc.

62852.d: Spain's next-generation military communications satellite, incorporating quantum-safe encryption, improved anti-jam capability, and significantly higher throughput than the original Spainsat. Spainsat NG I keeps Spain at the forefront of European military space capabilities — a strategic choice reflecting Spain's growing role in NATO and EU defense initiatives and its ambition to maintain sovereign communications for national security missions.

62876.d: A newer Michibiki satellite expanding Japan's quasi-zenith constellation. Japan plans to expand to seven Michibiki satellites to achieve redundant, always-available high-accuracy navigation across Japan and the Asia-Pacific region. Each additional satellite improves service availability and provides backup during maintenance of other constellation members — essential as Japan integrates Michibiki into critical infrastructure including aviation, maritime navigation, and emergency management systems.

63075.d: A Chinese replacement or supplementary communications satellite in the Zhongxing fleet. The 'R' suffix indicates a replenishment mission, ensuring continuity of service as older satellites approach end of life. China's systematic satellite replacement program demonstrates the commercial maturity of its satellite communications sector — managing a fleet across multiple generations rather than ad-hoc procurement.

63157.d: One of the newest TJS satellites, reflecting the ongoing prioritization of GEO military communications within China's space program. China's pace of classified GEO launches has raised concerns in the US Space Force and among allied nations about the nature and intentions of the TJS program, prompting increased space domain awareness investment by Western nations to better understand what these satellites are actually doing in orbit.

63361.d: A newer Tianlian second-generation relay satellite expanding China's orbital communications coverage. Tianlian 2-04 supports not only the Tiangong Space Station but China's growing fleet of commercial and government Earth observation satellites, which increasingly require high-rate downlink channels that the Tianlian system provides independently of ground station networks.

63397.d: A recent TJS satellite continuing China's classified GEO military program at an accelerating pace. TJS-16 is one of multiple TJS launches in 2024 alone, demonstrating China's commitment to rapidly expanding its protected military communications architecture in geostationary orbit as global competition in the space domain intensifies.

63524.d: One of the most recent TJS satellites, part of what has become China's most rapidly growing classified GEO program. The TJS series has become a central concern for US Space Force planners attempting to characterize China's geostationary military presence — understanding what these satellites actually do is critical to understanding the military balance in space.

63662.d: One of the newest Chinese data relay satellites, continuing the expansion of China's orbital communications infrastructure toward a full global constellation. Tianlian 2-05 represents China's sustained investment in the critical but unglamorous infrastructure that makes everything else in its space program possible — connecting crewed spacecraft, science satellites, and exploration missions to the ground continuously, regardless of where they are in their orbit.

63772.d: ESA's most unusual Earth observation satellite fires 70 cm radar wavelengths long enough to penetrate dense forest canopies and measure woody biomass. Biomass will produce the first global map of forest carbon stocks — the missing variable holding the largest uncertainty in climate science. If forests store twice as much carbon as we think, everything about climate policy needs revisiting.

63924.d: A newly launched TJS satellite extending China's classified GEO military constellation. The sheer number of TJS satellites — exceeding 20 by 2025 — and their observed proximity operations behavior represent a fundamental change in how China is approaching the geostationary domain: not merely as a communications medium but as an active operational environment where Chinese military capabilities must be continuously present.

64062.d: A Chinese communications satellite providing C and Ku-band capacity for government and commercial users. ChinaSat-3B serves China's growing satellite communications market, supporting government networks, enterprise data links, maritime and aviation connectivity, and regional broadcasting across the Asia-Pacific — a market where Chinese operators have been aggressively expanding at the expense of international competitors.

64290.d: One of SiriusXM's newest satellites, maintaining the robust multi-satellite architecture that delivers uninterrupted audio programming to tens of millions of subscribers. SXM-10's contribution to the constellation ensures that SiriusXM continues to provide a service uniquely suited to the mobile American lifestyle — entertainment that follows you from New York to Los Angeles without a single interruption or signal search.

64467.d: One of the most recent classified Russian military satellites in geostationary orbit. Launched in 2024 as Russia maintains pace with its military space modernization program, COSMOS 2589 represents Russia's continued investment in assured military communications in space — a capability seen as essential for command and control of Russian strategic forces and coordination of military operations across its global military presence.

64470.d: The latest ChinaSat-9 direct-to-home broadcasting satellite, providing next-generation HDTV and 4K content delivery across China. ChinaSat-9C supports China's national goal of transitioning its satellite TV broadcasting infrastructure to high-definition standards, reaching the hundreds of millions of Chinese viewers who receive television primarily via satellite dish rather than cable.

64694.d: Japan's next-generation greenhouse gas satellite combines CO2 and methane measurement with ocean wind sensing. It measures greenhouse gas concentrations precisely enough to verify whether national commitments under the Paris Agreement are being kept — a scientific fact-checker for climate diplomacy operating from orbit.

64723.d: Europe's first geostationary infrared sounder — a completely new capability for European meteorology. MTG-S1 doesn't image clouds; it measures temperature and humidity in 3D across Europe and Africa every hour in 3,600 spectral channels. Scientists compare it to replacing a 2D weather map with a 3D scan — the leap MRI brought to medicine, applied to the sky. Expected to extend severe storm warnings by 30 minutes.

64784.d: Israel's first dedicated satellite for the country's ultra-Orthodox Haredi community — providing specially curated television content without internet connectivity that meets strict religious content guidelines. Dror ('Freedom' in Hebrew) broadcasts kosher programming including news, religious content, and educational channels to an audience of over a million Israelis who observe strict media content standards. It occupies an unusual niche — perhaps the only satellite whose mission is defined by religious content governance rather than commercial or military requirements.

64876.d: Russia's third Ionosfera-M expands the constellation as solar maximum approaches with more frequent ionospheric disturbances. The multi-satellite network provides continuous monitoring needed to issue timely space weather warnings to Russia's power grid operators, aviation authorities, and military communications systems.

64877.d: The fourth Ionosfera-M completes Russia's basic operational ionospheric monitoring constellation. Four satellites at complementary inclinations can map the global ionosphere continuously — providing Russia the space weather infrastructure equivalent to what NOAA's Space Weather Prediction Center provides to the US and its allies.

64899.d: France's CNES MicroCarb measures atmospheric CO2 precisely enough to verify whether national emissions reductions reported under the Paris Agreement are actually occurring or merely on paper. 'Trust but verify' for climate policy, conducted from orbit by a satellite the size of a washing machine.

64900.d: One of four CO3D stereoscopic satellites forming France's global 3D mapping constellation. The system will produce a complete 3D model of Earth's entire land surface at 50 cm resolution — the definitive three-dimensional snapshot of Earth as it exists today, against which every future change will be measured.

64901.d: CO3D #4, part of the four-satellite stereoscopic system capturing simultaneous multi-angle imagery to reconstruct Earth's surface in 3D. Flying in precise formation, the constellation will complete a global mapping campaign in one year — replacing decades of patchwork terrain data with one coherent dataset.

64902.d: CO3D #2, part of the constellation that will be the first to image Earth's complete land surface in consistent high-resolution 3D. The dataset will serve as the baseline against which future elevation changes from climate change, human development, and geological processes will be measured for generations.

64903.d: The lead CO3D satellite, progenitor of a dataset that will be used for generations. CO3D-1's three-dimensional snapshot of Earth in the 2020s will eventually be compared to future surveys to document how the planet is changing — a benchmark for measuring Earth's transformation set in the current decade.

65055.d: Saudi Arabia's SAR reconnaissance satellite provides all-weather, day-night radar imaging of the Middle East. It extends Saudi sovereign surveillance to conditions where optical satellites are blind — through clouds, dust storms, and darkness — giving the Kingdom persistent situational awareness independent of any foreign satellite partner.

65159.d: Europe's next-generation polar weather satellite begins the transition to the most advanced operational weather observing system ever. Its Infrared Atmospheric Sounding Interferometer-Next Generation measures atmospheric temperature and humidity in 16,000 spectral channels — detecting molecular absorption signatures that reveal weather patterns before they develop into storms.

65316.d: Planet Labs' Pelican-4 — one of the fourth-generation imaging satellites representing Planet's sharpest eyes in orbit. The Pelican constellation is Planet's answer to Maxar and Airbus: a 30 cm resolution system capable of revisiting any location up to 30 times per day with a 32-satellite constellation. Each Pelican carries a Cassegrain telescope with a 4.03-meter effective focal length — capable of distinguishing an object the size of a laptop from 519 km up. Pelican-4 launched August 26, 2025 aboard a SpaceX Falcon-9 NAOS rideshare from Vandenberg. It was commissioned in late 2025 as part of the constellation that helped Planet turn its first profitable year as a company.
65316.t: Very High Resolution Commercial Imaging (30 cm)

65815.d: The most advanced Fengyun-3 polar satellite, FY-3H carries upgraded microwave and ozone instruments. Each successive Fengyun-3 demonstrates China's growing mastery — from copying Western instruments to pioneering unique orbital strategies like FY-3E's dawn-dusk orbit that no other nation has attempted operationally.

66052.d: A piece of Russian scientific hardware now drifting in its own orbit after being detached from the exterior of the International Space Station. On October 16, 2025, Roscosmos cosmonauts Kud-Sverchkov and Mikaev exited the ISS and retrieved this HRC (High Resolution Camera) monoblock — part of a compact radio-optical detector system mounted on the station's exterior to test Earth observation and ecological monitoring technology. After removal during the spacewalk, the unit was released from the ISS and catalogued as a separate orbiting object. It is now slowly descending, losing altitude to atmospheric drag until it re-enters. A piece of a telescope, orbiting alone, watching the Earth it was designed to photograph.

66142.d: One of the latest TJS satellites, launched in 2025 as China continues to expand its classified GEO military presence. The TJS program has now been active for a decade, and its scale has grown from a single experimental satellite to what analysts describe as a mature military communications and possibly signals intelligence constellation operating across the entire geostationary arc.

66311.d: India's replacement military naval communications satellite, updating the capability introduced by GSAT-7 with higher power, more frequency bands, and improved coverage of the Indian Ocean. GSAT-7R continues the Indian Navy's development of real-time maritime communications and situational awareness — essential for a navy that increasingly operates across the full width of the Indo-Pacific as India's strategic interests expand beyond traditional South Asian boundaries.

66454.d: The second ViaSat-3 satellite, covering Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Viasat-3 F2 learned from F1's reflector deployment issue, incorporating design modifications to reduce the same risk. Despite the F1 setback, Viasat pressed forward with its multi-satellite strategy, betting that its GEO high-throughput architecture can compete with LEO broadband constellations like Starlink in markets where the price and latency tradeoffs favor established GEO operators.

66514.d: Sentinel-6B continues the most precise sea level measurement record in history into the 2030s. The combined Sentinel-6A and 6B data will determine whether ocean rise is accelerating decade over decade — the key measurement for infrastructure planning in Miami, Shanghai, Amsterdam, Mumbai, and every other coastal city planning its future against a moving sea.

66549.d: A recent Shijian technology demonstration satellite testing advanced GEO capabilities. China's SJ series demonstrates Beijing's approach to military space technology development — test boldly in orbit with acknowledged technology demonstration satellites, then incorporate proven capabilities into operational systems. This approach differs from the US model of extensive ground testing before flight, allowing China to advance capabilities faster.

66655.d: Part of China's growing CAS500 commercial satellite family offering cost-effective 2-meter optical imaging. These platforms represent China's broader strategy to build commercial space capabilities that compete globally — developing the industrial ecosystem for full satellite self-sufficiency.

66906.d: Russian-origin hardware jettisoned from the International Space Station exterior during a 2025 EVA, now tracking independently in low Earth orbit. Objects like DUPLEX are a recurring artifact of the ISS Russian segment — experiments are mounted externally on Zvezda, Nauka, or Poisk modules, operated for their test duration, then removed during spacewalks and released into free orbit to gradually re-enter. The ISS has spawned hundreds of such tracked objects. DUPLEX, likely named for its dual-function design, has now completed its mission. It is silently descending toward an atmospheric re-entry of its own, a very small piece of a very large space station finding its own path back to Earth.

66990.d: Part of China's continuing TJS classified GEO satellite program. The rapid cadence of TJS launches — several per year through 2024 and 2025 — reflects China's strategic determination to achieve parity with the United States in geostationary military capability. Each new TJS satellite makes China's space-based military architecture more resilient, redundant, and difficult to counter.

67226.d: One of the newest TJS satellites. Chinese officials have not acknowledged the military nature of the TJS series, but the operational pace and scale of the program, combined with observed anomalous behaviors, have convinced US Space Force and independent analysts that TJS represents a major Chinese military GEO capability development program rather than any experimental communications initiative.

67302.d: One of two Shijian-29 satellites launched together, demonstrating China's growing capability to deploy complex paired GEO systems. Twin GEO technology demonstrators allow China to test formation flying, inter-satellite links, and distributed payload architectures — capabilities essential for both future military GEO constellations and next-generation commercial communications systems.

67304.d: The third COSMO-SkyMed Second Generation satellite gives Italy sub-4-hour revisit over Mediterranean targets — the capability to provide near-continuous monitoring of the world's most-watched waterway for illegal migration, arms smuggling, and strategic naval movement.

67403.d: The companion to Shijian-29A, demonstrating China's twin-satellite GEO technology testing approach. Flying two identical platforms in close orbital proximity allows Chinese engineers to test distributed computing across spacecraft, formation control algorithms, and coordinated observation — capabilities that would be directly applicable to a military constellation that needs satellites to work cooperatively.

67684.d: A 2U CubeSat developed by Sapienza University of Rome and deployed from the ISS's Japanese Kibo module via JAXA's J-SSOD #35 small satellite deployer in October 2025. CORAL represents the Italian university space program at its most tangible — student-built hardware ejected from the same airlock that has launched hundreds of CubeSats from 17 countries over the past decade. The ISS Kibo module has become the world's most accessible satellite launch platform, allowing universities to deploy directly from 400 km altitude without booking a dedicated launch. It shares its orbit with the station that built it.

67757.d: Iran's satellite broadcasting service, named after Jam-e Jam — the mythical cup of King Jamshid in Persian mythology that was said to reflect the entire world. Jam-e Jam is also Iran's international state broadcasting brand. The satellite provides Iranian state television programming across the Middle East, Europe, and Central Asia — an instrument of Iranian soft power, projecting Farsi-language news, cultural programming, and political messaging to diaspora communities and international audiences.

68835.d: An advanced optical Earth observation satellite in Saudi Arabia's sovereign program. Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 explicitly targets domestic satellite manufacturing — and the Kingdom's investment in operational reconnaissance satellites is building the engineering base to eventually achieve it.

69013.d: China's CAS500-2 commercial observation platform demonstrates China's ability to offer reliable mid-resolution Earth observation at competitive prices. As Chinese satellite manufacturers enter the global market for commercial platforms, they are following the path of Chinese electronics — from domestic supply chain to world export.

69123.d: Launched just weeks ago in May 2026, SMILE is humanity's first attempt to photograph Earth's magnetic shield in X-rays — imaging the magnetosphere the way a doctor images a body. This ESA-Chinese joint mission will map how the boundary of Earth's magnetic field moves and flexes in response to solar wind for up to 40 continuous hours per orbit, using a soft X-ray imager and UV aurora camera simultaneously. It will answer a deceptively simple question we've never been able to answer before: what does Earth's magnetic shield actually look like from the outside?

69235.d: One of the most recently launched TJS satellites, continuing China's relentless expansion of its classified GEO military program. The TJS constellation now spans the geostationary arc with multiple satellites providing overlapping coverage — an architecture that provides redundancy against both technical failures and potential adversary interference. TJS-24 represents the current frontier of China's military GEO presence.

07646.d: The world's oldest actively tracked geodetic satellite — a solid brass sphere 24 cm across launched in 1975 and still flying. Laser ranging stations fire pulses at its 60 retroreflectors and time the returning light to millimeter precision, measuring how Earth's gravitational field changes as ice sheets melt and water moves around the planet. Still delivering science after 50 years in orbit.

08820.d: A 60 cm brass sphere covered in 426 retroreflectors, LAGEOS-1 is the most precisely tracked object ever placed in orbit. Ground stations bounce laser pulses off it to measure distance within millimeters — tracking the slow drift of tectonic plates, the tiny wobble of Earth's axis, and how melting ice sheets redistribute Earth's mass. Launched in 1976, it carries a plaque showing Earth's continents as they'll look in 8 million years. LAGEOS is designed to survive that long.

05560.d: A US Air Force Space Test Program satellite launched in 1971 that is still in orbit 55 years later — one of the oldest tracked objects in low Earth orbit that has not re-entered. ASTEX (SESP 1971-2) tested a celestial mapping infrared scanner, a 457-meter flexible roll-up solar array (the largest ever flown at the time), and instruments measuring energetic particle interactions with the ionosphere. The satellite worked until December 20, 1973, when a command telling the transmitter to stop was issued and failed — the transmitter kept broadcasting until it wore itself out. The spacecraft went silent in 1973 but never came down. It circles Earth today at 720 km altitude, a 50-year-old relic of Nixon-era space technology that has outlasted every mission it was ever built to support.

04327.d: The satellite that proved ion propulsion works in space — and is still up there 56 years later. SERT 2 (Space Electric Rocket Test 2), launched February 3, 1970, carried two mercury ion thrusters and the largest solar arrays NASA had ever flown, operating them for thousands of hours in the first long-duration electric propulsion test in history. One thruster ran 2,011 hours before a grid short stopped it; the second ran 3,781 hours. NASA reactivated the thrusters periodically through 1981, completing over 500 restarts. The technology validated here is the direct ancestor of the ion propulsion systems now used for station-keeping on virtually every commercial geostationary satellite and for deep-space missions like Dawn, Hayabusa, and BepiColombo. The spacecraft itself, now silent, continues to orbit Earth — the great-grandfather of modern electric propulsion, still flying.
04327.t: Ion Propulsion Pioneer (Silent — Still Orbiting After 55 Years)
